title: Wilson's War Message to Congress
text:
Gentlemen of the Congress: I have called the Congress into extraordinary session because there are serious, very serious, choices of policy to be made, and made immediately, which it was neither right nor constitutionally permissible that I should assume the responsibility of making.

On the third of February last I officially laid before you the extraordinary announcement of the Imperial German Government that on and after the first day of February it was its purpose to put aside all restraints of law or of humanity and use its submarines to sink every vessel that sought to approach either the ports of Great Britain and Ireland or the western coasts of Europe or any of the ports controlled by the enemies of Germany within the Mediterranean.

The new policy has swept every restriction aside. Vessels of every kind, whatever their flag, their character, their cargo, their destination, their errand, have been ruthlessly sent to the bottom without warning and without thought of help or mercy for those on board. Even hospital ships and ships carrying relief to the sorely bereaved and stricken people of Belgium have been sunk with the same reckless lack of compassion or of principle.

With a profound sense of the solemn and even tragical character of the step I am taking and of the grave responsibilities which it involves, but in unhesitating obedience to what I deem my constitutional duty, I advise that the Congress declare the recent course of the Imperial German Government to be in fact nothing less than war against the government and people of the United States.

It is a fearful thing to lead this great peaceful people into war, into the most disastrous and dismal of all wars, civilization itself seeming to be in the balance. But the right is more precious than peace, and we shall fight for the things which we have always carried nearest our hearts — for democracy, for the right of those who submit to authority to have a voice in their own governments, for the rights and liberties of small nations, for a universal dominion of right by such a concert of free peoples as shall bring peace and safety to all nations and make the world itself at last free.

To such a task we can dedicate our lives and our fortunes, everything that we are and everything that we have, with the pride of those who know that the day has come when America is privileged to spend her blood and her might for the principles that gave her birth and happiness and the peace which she has treasured. God helping her, she can do no other.

title: Wilson's Fourteen Points Address to Congress
text:
Gentlemen of the Congress: Once more, as repeatedly before, the spokesmen of the Central Empires have indicated their desire to discuss the objects of the war and the possible bases of a general peace.

We entered this war because violations of right had occurred which touched us to the quick and made the life of our own people impossible unless they were corrected and the world secured once for all against their recurrence. What we demand in this war is nothing peculiar to ourselves. It is that the world be made fit and safe to live in; particularly that it be made safe for every peace-loving nation which wishes to live its own life, determine its own institutions, be assured of justice and fair dealing by other peoples as against force and selfish aggression.

The program of the world's peace is our program:

I. Open covenants of peace, openly arrived at, after which there shall be no private international understandings of any kind.

II. Absolute freedom of navigation upon the seas, outside territorial waters, alike in peace and in war.

III. The removal of all economic barriers and the establishment of an equality of trade conditions among all nations consenting to the peace.

IV. Adequate guarantees that national armaments will be reduced to the lowest point consistent with domestic safety.

V. An impartial adjustment of all colonial claims, based upon the principle that the interests of the populations concerned must have equal weight with the claims of the government whose title is to be determined.

VI. The evacuation of all Russian territory and a settlement of all questions affecting Russia as will secure her an unhampered opportunity for the independent determination of her own political development.

VII. Belgium must be evacuated and restored, without any attempt to limit the sovereignty which she enjoys in common with all other free nations.

VIII. All French territory should be freed and the invaded portions restored, and the wrong done to France by Prussia in 1871 in the matter of Alsace-Lorraine should be righted.

IX. A readjustment of the frontiers of Italy should be effected along clearly recognizable lines of nationality.

X. The peoples of Austria-Hungary should be accorded the freest opportunity of autonomous development.

XI. Romania, Serbia, and Montenegro should be evacuated and Serbia accorded free and secure access to the sea.

XII. The Turkish portions of the Ottoman Empire should be assured a secure sovereignty, but other nationalities under Turkish rule should be assured security of life and autonomous development.

XIII. An independent Polish state should be erected which should include territories inhabited by indisputably Polish populations, with free and secure access to the sea.

XIV. A general association of nations must be formed under specific covenants for the purpose of affording mutual guarantees of political independence and territorial integrity to great and small states alike.

An evident principle runs through the whole program I have outlined. It is the principle of justice to all peoples and nationalities, and their right to live on equal terms of liberty and safety with one another, whether they be strong or weak. The people of the United States could act upon no other principle; and to the vindication of this principle they are ready to devote their lives, their honor, and everything that they possess.

title: Prime Minister Asquith's Address to Parliament on the Declaration of War
source: First World War Primary Documents Archive
text:
With the utmost reluctance and with infinite regret, His Majesty's Government have been compelled to put this country in a state of war with what for many years and indeed generations past has been a friendly Power.

The Papers which have since been presented to Parliament will show how strenuous, how unremitting, how persistent were our efforts to preserve the peace of Europe. They will show, too, that we were in complete sympathy with the general wish of the Great Powers, that a conflict between two of their number should not involve the rest of Europe in war.

We have great and vital interests in the independence of Belgium, and we could not see with indifference that country absorbed by a great military Power. The House has read, and the country has read, in the last few hours, the most pathetic appeal addressed by the King of Belgium, and I do not envy the man who can read that appeal with an unmoved heart. Belgians are fighting and losing their lives. What would have been the position of Great Britain today, in the face of that spectacle, if we had assented to this infamous proposal?

Yes, and what are we to get in return for the betrayal of our friends and the dishonour of our obligations? A promise — nothing more; a promise given by a Power which was at that very moment announcing its intention to violate its most solemn international obligation.

I do not think that any nation in the history of the world could have confronted a more difficult or a more critical situation than that which faced the Government of this country during the last few days. We made every effort to find any alternative means of preserving peace without dishonour, without the betrayal of our obligations and our friends.

We could not, consistent with our national honour, our treaty obligations, and the vital interests of this country, do otherwise than take up this challenge. With a full conviction not only of the wisdom and justice, but of the obligations which lay upon us to challenge this great issue, we are entering into the struggle. Let us now make sure that all the resources, not only of this United Kingdom, but of the vast Empire of which it is the centre, shall be thrown into the scale.

We have great issues at stake — the protection of small nations, the sanctity of international treaties, and the defence of the liberties of Europe against the domination of a militaristic power that has shown itself willing to override every obligation of law and humanity in pursuit of conquest.

Britain enters this war not as an aggressor, not as a nation seeking gain, but as a nation fulfilling its treaty pledges and defending the principle that the rights of small nations must be respected and that no power shall be allowed to dominate Europe by force of arms alone.

title: King George V's Message to the British Expeditionary Force
source: First World War Primary Documents Archive
text:
My message to the troops of the Expeditionary Force.

You are leaving home to fight for the safety and honour of my Empire. Belgium, whose country we are pledged to defend, has been attacked and France is about to be invaded by the same powerful foe.

I have implicit confidence in you my soldiers. Duty is your watchword, and I know your duty will be nobly done.

I shall follow your every movement with deepest interest and mark with eager satisfaction your daily progress, indeed your welfare will never be absent from my thoughts.

I pray God to bless you and guard you and bring you back victorious.

George R.I.

The British Expeditionary Force departed in August 1914 to fulfil Britain's treaty obligations to Belgium and France. King George V's message reflects the spirit of duty, honour, and loyalty to allied nations that defined the British entry into the Great War.

Belgium had been guaranteed neutrality under the Treaty of London of 1839, a treaty signed by the major European powers including Great Britain and Prussia. When German forces crossed into Belgian territory on August 4, 1914, Great Britain regarded this as not only a violation of international law but a direct challenge to the security and honour of its Empire.

The British Expeditionary Force, composed of regular professional soldiers, was dispatched immediately to support French and Belgian forces along the Western Front. Their courage and discipline in the early months of the war, particularly at the Battle of Mons and during the retreat to the Marne, helped to slow the German advance and buy vital time for the Allies to consolidate their defensive lines.

King George V remained deeply engaged with the progress of the war throughout its duration, visiting troops in France and maintaining correspondence with commanders in the field. His message to the departing BEF was intended both as a personal expression of royal confidence and as a public declaration that the Empire stood united behind its soldiers in the field.

The courage of these men, who answered the call of duty without hesitation, would lay the foundation for the eventual Allied victory in 1918.

title: France's Declaration of War Against Germany
source: World War I Document Archive, Brigham Young University
text:
France has just been the object of a violent and premeditated attack, which is an insolent defiance of the law of nations. Before any declaration of war had been sent to us, before I had even addressed the German Emperor in a personal appeal for the maintenance of peace, Germany, without warning, has made a sudden onslaught on our territory, in the air and on land. France has no responsibility for this war. She has done everything in her power to prevent it. We have given proof of our sincere desire for peace. We submitted to the humiliation of the Agadir crisis. We endured the occupation of Morocco. We accepted conditions after conditions imposed on us.

For over forty years we have kept in our hearts the wound of 1871. We have patiently rebuilt our strength. We have sought alliances to ensure the balance of power and the peace of Europe. We have not sought revenge — we have sought security. But there is a limit to patience, and that limit has been reached.

Germany has chosen war. Germany has invaded neutral Belgium in defiance of solemn treaty obligations, obligations which she herself signed and to which she was morally bound. Germany has struck the first blow against civilization itself. In doing so, she has united not only France but all nations that cherish law, liberty, and the rights of peoples to live free from military domination.

France rises now, not in anger alone, but in the consciousness of her duty to herself and to humanity. Our cause is the cause of honor, of justice, and of civilization. The French people, united in a Sacred Union that transcends all party differences, will prosecute this war with all the energy and determination at their command. We fight for the liberation of those who have suffered under the German yoke, for the restoration of Alsace-Lorraine to the Fatherland, and for the construction of a lasting peace in Europe founded on justice and the rights of all peoples.

France calls upon her sons. France calls upon her allies. France calls upon all peoples of goodwill. Together we shall resist this aggression, and together we shall prevail. The righteousness of our cause is our greatest strength, and history shall vindicate us.

title: British Foreign Secretary Sir Edward Grey's Speech to Parliament on the Eve of War
source: World War I Document Archive, Brigham Young University
text:
Last week I stated that we were working for peace not only for this country, but to preserve the peace of Europe. Today events move so rapidly that it is exceedingly difficult for anyone to state exactly what is the situation. Yesterday afternoon I gave to the House some information which, I think, satisfied them that, if the matter had been one which concerned this country and Germany alone, we had at any rate secured delay and we were still in a position to come out without having our credit compromised or our honor stained. The moment, however, that issue arose and Belgium came in, a new and more vital issue was raised.

We had great and vital interests in the independence of Belgium. We have had them for generations. The independence of Belgium, the freedom of the Netherlands, these have been vital interests of Great Britain for three hundred years. The neutrality of Belgium, guaranteed by a treaty to which Great Britain, France, and Germany are all parties, is not simply a legal obligation. It is the foundation stone of the peaceful settlement of Europe. To violate it is not merely to break a treaty — it is to repudiate the entire basis upon which the peace of Europe has rested.

Germany has demanded free passage through Belgium. Belgium has refused. Germany has invaded Belgium. This is a fact which changes everything. It is not simply the invasion of a small country. It is the deliberate destruction of the system of international law and treaty obligation which alone stands between civilization and the law of the jungle.

We must face this situation. If we stand aside, we lose our honor. We lose our position in the world. We lose the respect of our allies and the confidence of neutral nations. We shall be regarded not merely as cowards but as accomplices in the destruction of international law. The consequences of such a failure would not be confined to the present war. They would poison the wells of peace for generations to come.

I ask the House to consider what the situation would be if we surrendered that tremendous interest and that obligation. We are going to suffer, I am afraid, terribly in this war. But the honor and security of this country and the future of civilization require that we meet this challenge with courage and resolution.

title: Australian Prime Minister Andrew Fisher's Pledge of Support to Britain
text:
Australians stand behind the Mother Country to the last man and the last shilling. The liberty and freedom which we enjoy are the outcome of the struggles of our forefathers — struggles waged in defense of those principles which have made the British Empire the guardian of human rights and the champion of oppressed peoples throughout the world. It is not only a duty but a privilege for Australia to contribute to the defense of the Empire.

The cause for which we fight is a just and noble one. Germany has plunged Europe into war through reckless militarism and a reckless disregard for the rights of smaller nations. The invasion of Belgium is an act of naked aggression which no civilized nation can condone. That a great power should march its armies across a neutral country, in defiance of solemn treaty obligations, is a crime against international law and against humanity itself.

Australia is a young nation, but we are heirs to a great tradition. We have built our Commonwealth on the values of freedom, fair play, and respect for law. These are not merely British values — they are universal values, and they are now under attack. Australia cannot stand aside while those values are trampled underfoot by Prussian militarism.

Our young men will answer the call gladly and with pride. They will go forward as soldiers of the King and of the Empire, but also as defenders of all that is best in human civilization. They will show the world that the British race, wherever it is found, has lost none of its fighting spirit or its determination to stand for what is right.

We do not seek conquest. We do not seek territory. We seek only the restoration of peace and justice in a world threatened by tyranny. When victory is won, it will be a victory not for Britain or for Australia alone, but for all peoples who love freedom and who wish to live their lives without fear of military domination. Australia pledges herself fully to this cause and will not waver until final victory is achieved.

title: French President Poincare's Address to Parliament on the Declaration of War
text:
France has just been the object of a violent and premeditated attack, which is an insolent defiance of the law of nations. Before any declaration of war had been sent to us, before we had even received the German Ambassador, our territory has been violated. The German Empire has waited only until the German Ambassador had demanded his passports to launch this assault upon us.

France has not provoked this war. She has done everything to prevent it. She has given proof of her moderation and submitted to the heaviest sacrifices in order to avert the present conflict. Since she could not prevent it, she takes it up. She did not choose war. War was imposed upon her.

France is not alone in this struggle. She has the support of her ally, Russia, and the sympathies of all free peoples who love justice and respect the rights of nations. Great Britain, faithful to her treaty obligations and to the principles of international law, has also entered the conflict at our side.

Our country is not responsible for this war. France goes into battle for honor, for the liberation of invaded territory, for the defence of right against brutal force. She goes to war firm in the conviction that she fights not only for France but for civilization itself.

The invaded provinces of Alsace and Lorraine, torn from the Motherland by force in 1871, have never ceased to belong to France in spirit and in the hearts of their people. The hour has come when France may answer the call of those provinces, and the call of all peoples who believe that nations have the right to determine their own fate.

To all Frenchmen, whatever their political opinion, whatever their region, whatever their walk of life, France speaks with one voice today: rise up, defend the Republic, defend the Motherland. The enemy has left us no other choice. But we shall face this trial with the calm resolution of a nation conscious of its right, and we shall prevail.

France has been attacked. France will defend herself. France will be victorious. And the peace that follows this war shall be a peace of justice, a peace that secures for all peoples the freedom and dignity that Germany has today sought to destroy.

title: Field Marshal Sir John French's Dispatch on the Battle of Mons
text:
I have the honour to report the proceedings of the British Expeditionary Force from August 22nd to September 5th, 1914.

In accordance with the instructions I received, the British Forces took up their position in the area of Mons on the 22nd and 23rd of August. The troops were disposed along the line of the canal between Mons and Conde, and were prepared to offer resistance to any German advance from the north.

On the morning of the 23rd August, the enemy attacked in great force. The German infantry advanced in dense masses across the open ground north of the canal, suffering terrible losses from the accurate rifle and machine-gun fire of the British infantry. The conduct of the troops throughout this engagement was magnificent. Though heavily outnumbered, they held their positions against repeated attacks with a steadiness and determination that reflected the highest credit upon themselves and upon the British Army.

Despite the heroic resistance of the British forces at Mons, the retirement of the French armies on both flanks made it impossible for the BEF to maintain its position. Under the most difficult conditions, the retreat from Mons was conducted with great skill and discipline. The rearguard actions fought during the retreat demonstrated the fighting qualities of British soldiers to the highest degree.

The Battle of the Marne, fought in early September, brought the retreat to an end. The Allied counterattack, launched on the 6th of September, struck the German First Army in a vulnerable position and forced a general German withdrawal to the Aisne. The spirit and energy with which British troops participated in this advance reflects the finest traditions of the British Army.

I desire to express my admiration of and gratitude to the officers commanding the Army Corps under my command, and to record my appreciation of the magnificent behavior of the troops throughout these operations. Fighting against superior numbers, often exhausted by long marches and with insufficient rest, they never failed in courage or in discipline.

The losses sustained by the British Expeditionary Force during this period have been serious, but they have not been in vain. The enemy has been checked. The line has been held. And the sacrifice of those who fell at Mons and during the retreat shall be honored by ultimate victory.

title: Lloyd George's Speech on the War Aims of Great Britain
text:
We are not fighting a war of aggression against the German people. We have repeatedly declared that we are not fighting to destroy Germany or to partition her territory, or to impose upon her people any form of government contrary to their will. We are not fighting to deprive Germany of her rightful place among the nations of Europe or of the world.

But we are fighting to establish the right of nations to govern themselves, free from the arbitrary decisions of autocratic military establishments. We are fighting to prove that the deliberate violation of international treaties, the invasion and occupation of neutral countries, the deliberate terrorizing of civilian populations, and the wholesale destruction of civilian property cannot be accepted as the normal instruments of national policy.

The principles for which we are fighting are these:

First, the restoration of Belgium and the reparation of the wrong done to that country, whose neutrality Germany deliberately violated in defiance of a treaty to which Germany herself was a party.

Second, the restoration of Serbia, Montenegro, and the occupied portions of France, Italy, and Romania.

Third, a reconsideration of the great settlement of 1871, in which the wishes of the people of Alsace-Lorraine were never consulted by those who disposed of them.

Fourth, a recognition of the principle of nationality and of the right of all people to self-determination. This means that we stand by the side of the oppressed peoples of Europe, whatever their race, their religion, or their language.

Fifth, the creation after the war of some international organization to limit the burden of armaments and diminish the probability of war.

We believe that these are aims which all free peoples can support. We do not seek a vindictive peace. We seek a just peace — a peace that will endure because it rests upon the consent of the peoples governed, and because it removes the grievances which make nations willing to go to war.

But until a peace based upon these principles is achieved, Great Britain and her allies will continue to prosecute this war with every resource at their command.

title: General Haig's Despatch on the Battle of the Somme
source: First World War Primary Documents Archive
text:
The operations on the Somme were commenced on the 1st July with the object of relieving the pressure on the French at Verdun and of co-operating in a general offensive which had been arranged with our Allies. The results of these operations have been of the greatest value in assisting our Allies and in the destruction of the enemy's man-power and material.

In the weeks that followed, the British forces, supported by our gallant French allies, pressed forward with unrelenting determination against an enemy who defended every yard of ground with the full resources at his disposal. The German soldier fought with the stubbornness that we have come to expect of him, but our troops displayed a courage and endurance that has written new and glorious pages in the history of the British Army.

The Battle of the Somme has destroyed the old German Army. By this I mean that portion of the Kaiser's forces which had been carefully trained in Germany. We have inflicted enormous losses on the enemy and we have reduced his reserves. The work has been done. The enemy has been weakened. We have proved beyond all doubt that our troops can beat the German Army in open fighting, can take their positions, hold them, and advance again.

The heroism of our men has never been surpassed. They attacked through a hail of machine-gun fire and artillery bombardment. They took their objectives with a steadiness and determination that filled every observer with admiration. Though the casualties have been heavy — and no honest account could conceal this — they have been no heavier than the nature of the operations required, and they have been repaid in a measure fully equal to their cost in the progress of the Allied cause.

The enemy is exhausted. His reserves have been used up. His best divisions have been thrown into the Somme battle and have been ground down. The lessons of this great battle will serve to train and inspire our armies for the final effort that shall bring victory. We have learned much. We have grown stronger. The foundations of ultimate triumph have been laid on the Somme.

I desire to place on record my deep appreciation of the loyal and devoted services rendered by all ranks of the British Armies during the Somme operations. They have borne cheerfully the hardships and dangers inseparable from so prolonged and arduous a battle, and their steadfast courage has been an inspiration to the cause of the Allies throughout the world.

title: Marshal Foch's Declaration on the Armistice Terms
source: First World War Primary Documents Archive
text:
At eleven o'clock this morning the guns fell silent along the Western Front. After more than four years of the most terrible and destructive war in the history of mankind, the armies of Germany have laid down their arms. The Armistice is signed. The war is over.

This victory has been won by the combined effort of all the Allied nations — France, Great Britain, the United States, Belgium, Italy, and all those who stood with us in the defense of civilization against military aggression. It has been won by the courage and endurance of millions of soldiers who fought and suffered and died in the belief that their sacrifice would make the world a better and safer place.

The terms of the Armistice which the German representatives have signed compel the immediate evacuation of all occupied territories — Belgium, France, Alsace-Lorraine — and the surrender of vast quantities of military material. The German Army, which four years ago swept through Belgium in contempt of every treaty obligation, retires now in defeat. Justice has been done.

But we must not be blinded by victory to the magnitude of what has been lost. The flower of a generation lies in the fields of Flanders and the Marne, of the Somme and Verdun, of the Aisne and the Chemin des Dames. France alone has lost more than a million of her sons. The British Empire has given nearly a million dead. The cost has been incalculable.

The victory we have won must therefore be used wisely. The peace that follows must be a peace that makes this sacrifice meaningful — a peace based upon justice, upon the rights of nations great and small, upon the principles for which our soldiers died. If the peace is well made, then their deaths will not have been in vain. The future of civilization depends upon what we do now with the victory that has been so dearly bought.

France gives thanks this day for her liberation. Alsace and Lorraine, after forty-seven years of separation, return to the embrace of the mother country. The tricolor flies again over Strasbourg. The honor of France is restored. But our first thought today is for those who did not live to see this day.

title: General Pershing's Report on the American Expeditionary Forces
text:
The American Expeditionary Forces arrived in France in the summer of 1917 at a moment when the Allied cause stood in grave danger. The Russian army had collapsed. The French army had suffered mutinies following the failure of the Nivelle Offensive. The British forces were exhausted by three years of continuous fighting. The submarines were threatening to cut the supply lines across the Atlantic. The entry of the United States into the war, and above all the arrival of American troops in France in ever-increasing numbers, transformed the strategic situation.

The American soldier proved himself in every engagement in which he was committed. At Belleau Wood, at Chateau-Thierry, at Saint-Mihiel, and in the Meuse-Argonne Offensive, the soldiers of the American Expeditionary Forces demonstrated courage, determination, and fighting quality that won the admiration of our Allies and the respect of our enemies. The Meuse-Argonne Offensive, the greatest military operation in American history, engaged more than a million American soldiers and drove the German Army back continuously from September 26 to November 11, contributing decisively to the final Allied victory.

The American soldier fights best when he understands what he is fighting for. Our men understood. They fought for the principles that President Wilson had articulated — for democracy, for the rights of small nations, for a peace based upon justice rather than upon conquest. They fought because they believed that the cause of the Allies was the cause of humanity. Their belief was justified by the victory they helped to achieve.

The cost has been heavy. More than fifty thousand American soldiers gave their lives in battle. More than two hundred thousand were wounded. They died far from home, on foreign soil, for a cause they had freely chosen to defend. The nation owes them a debt that can never be fully repaid. The best repayment we can offer is to build, from the victory they helped to win, a world in which such a war can never happen again.

The American Expeditionary Forces return home with pride in what they have accomplished and with gratitude to the French and British comrades who fought beside them. The alliance forged in battle is an alliance that should endure in peace.

title: Belgian King Albert's Appeal to the Nation
source: First World War Primary Documents Archive
text:
Belgians,

The great powers of Europe are at war. Belgium, a neutral country, guaranteed by solemn treaties signed by all the great powers, finds herself today the victim of a brutal aggression. Germany, in defiance of every obligation of law and honour, has demanded that we open our territory to the passage of her armies. We have refused. Belgium could not do otherwise without sacrificing her honour and her independence.

Our duty is clear. We must resist. The whole world knows that Belgium has done nothing to provoke this attack. The whole world knows that we have been a peaceful, industrious people, faithful to our international obligations, seeking friendship with all our neighbours. We have been rewarded for our faithfulness by an attack that has no justification in law, in justice, or in humanity.

The Belgian Army takes up its position with calm and resolution. Our soldiers know what they are defending. They are defending their homes, their families, and the sacred soil of their country. They are defending the principle that a small nation has the right to exist, the right to independence, and the right to the protection of the treaties by which that independence was guaranteed.

I appeal to every Belgian citizen to do his duty. The soldier must fight. The worker must work to sustain the defense. Every man and woman must contribute what they can to the resistance of our country against this unjust and unprovoked attack.

Belgium did not seek this trial. It has been forced upon us. But we will face it with the courage and dignity that our history demands. A nation that was born in struggle, that won its independence through sacrifice, knows how to defend that independence when it is threatened.

To our Allies — to France, to Great Britain, to all those who honor the law of nations — Belgium appeals for the support that treaty and justice require. We do not fight alone. We fight in the company of all who believe that law must prevail over force and that the rights of small nations must be respected by the powerful.

Belgium will endure. Belgium will be free.

title: Field Marshal Sir Douglas Haig's Final Despatch on the Victory of 1918
source: First World War Primary Documents Archive
text:
The war has ended in the complete defeat of the enemy. The German Army, which four years ago swept through Belgium and threatened Paris, has been driven back across the Rhine and has laid down its arms. The victory is total and the cause for which our men fought and died has been vindicated before the world.

I desire to record the outstanding features of the campaign of 1918 which led to this final result. After the failure of the German Spring Offensives — which despite their initial tactical success never achieved the strategic breakthrough that alone could have won the war for Germany — the initiative passed to the Allied armies. On the 8th of August, what I believe history will record as the black day of the German Army, our forces struck at Amiens with a power and precision that shattered the German front and began the advance that never stopped until the Armistice.

The campaign of the Hundred Days, from the 8th of August to the 11th of November, was the greatest sustained offensive operation in British military history. Our forces, together with our French, American, Belgian, and Dominion allies, advanced continuously against an enemy who fought with skill and determination even in retreat. We took more than 188,000 prisoners and captured more than 2,800 guns in these final months of the war. These are the statistics of a decisive and overwhelming victory.

The victory was won by the courage and professionalism of the British soldier and his allies. It was won also by the development, over four years of war, of a military organization, a system of logistics, a body of tactical doctrine, and a quality of junior leadership that by 1918 was superior to anything Germany could put in the field. We had learned how to fight this new kind of war. The learning had been costly. But it had been done.

I commend to the nation the memory of those who gave their lives in the service of their country and the cause of civilization. Their sacrifice was not in vain. The world for which they died is not yet perfect, but it is free from the military domination that threatened to extinguish the liberty of nations. That is the achievement of the British Army in the Great War, and it will endure in history.

title: Australian Prime Minister Hughes on the ANZAC Sacrifice
text:
Today we commemorate for the first time the landing at Gallipoli. It is a day that will be remembered in Australia as long as the nation endures. On the 25th of April 1915, Australian and New Zealand soldiers stormed the beaches of the Gallipoli Peninsula and wrote in their blood one of the most heroic chapters in the history of any nation.

The men who landed at Anzac Cove were not professional soldiers. They were farmers and stockmen, miners and factory workers, clerks and tradesmen — the ordinary men of Australia who had answered the call of their country and their Empire without hesitation. They faced conditions of extraordinary difficulty. The beaches were narrow, the cliffs were steep, the enemy was entrenched on the high ground, and the fire was withering from the first moment of the landing. They pressed forward nonetheless, with a courage and determination that astonished the world.

The Gallipoli campaign did not achieve its strategic objectives. The Dardanelles were not forced. Constantinople was not threatened. But it achieved something more lasting and more important than any strategic objective. It revealed to the world, and above all to Australia herself, what Australian men are made of. It created in the trial of fire and blood a national character and a national tradition that will endure for generations.

The men who died at Gallipoli, and those who died afterwards in the mud of the Western Front and in the deserts of Sinai and Palestine, did not die for nothing. They died for Australia. They died for the Empire. They died for the principle that a nation of free men will always, when the moment demands it, stand up to be counted in the defense of freedom. Australia has been counted. Australia has paid the price. And Australia is proud.

We carry their memory with us today and always. We shall not forget them. At the going down of the sun and in the morning, we will remember them.

title: Italian Declaration of War on Austria-Hungary
text:
The Italian Government to the Imperial and Royal Austro-Hungarian Government:

Italy has observed with deep concern the manner in which Austria-Hungary has conducted its affairs with Serbia and the manner in which the war that has resulted has been extended across Europe. Italy, as a member of the Triple Alliance, was never consulted by Austria-Hungary before the ultimatum was delivered to Serbia that triggered the present conflict. Italy was presented with a fait accompli. This was a violation of the terms of the Alliance itself, which required prior consultation before any action likely to disturb the general peace.

Italy therefore declared its neutrality at the outset of the war on the grounds that the war as initiated by Austria-Hungary was a war of aggression, not the defensive war that alone would have required Italian participation under the terms of the Alliance. This position Italy has maintained for nine months while seeking, through negotiation, the satisfaction of Italian national aspirations which the Alliance had promised but never delivered.

Austria-Hungary has refused to give Italy the satisfaction to which it is entitled by the terms of Article VII of the Triple Alliance Treaty with respect to compensation for Austrian territorial gains in the Balkans. Austria-Hungary has refused to acknowledge Italian national rights in the unredeemed territories inhabited by Italian populations. Austria-Hungary has failed in every obligation to its ally.

Italy therefore finds itself compelled by duty to its national interests and to the aspirations of the Italian people to enter the present war on the side of those powers who have shown themselves willing to acknowledge the principles of nationality and the rights of peoples to self-determination. Italy enters this war not as a conqueror but as a nation reclaiming what is rightfully hers — the completion of the national unification that has been the aspiration of the Italian people for generations.

The Italian Army will fight with the valor that history has always shown to be the inheritance of the sons of Rome. Italy will not lay down its arms until the unredeemed territories are redeemed and the honor of the Italian nation is fully secured.

title: German Chancellor Bethmann-Hollweg's Speech to the Reichstag on the Outbreak of War
source: First World War Primary Documents Archive
text:
A mighty fate has descended upon Europe. Because we were struggling for the esteem of the German Empire in the world, we have for forty-four years lived in peace and safeguarded the peace of Europe. In peaceful industry we have become strong and mighty and in consequence envied. With patience we have borne that, under the pretext that Germany was desirous of war, hostility toward us was being nursed and chains forged for us both in the East and in the West. We wished to continue to live in peaceful industry, and, like an unexpressed vow, there was passed on from Kaiser to the youngest soldier: Only in defense of a righteous cause shall our sword be drawn.

The day when we must draw it has appeared, contrary to our desire, contrary to our honest efforts to avoid it. Russia has set fire to the building. We are at war with Russia and France — a war that has been forced upon us.

Where the responsibility in this greatest of all wars lies is quite evident to us. Outwardly responsible are the men in Russia who planned and carried into effect the general mobilization of the Russian army. But in reality and truth the British Government is responsible. The London Cabinet could have made war impossible if they had unequivocally told Petersburg that England was not willing to let a continental war of the Great Powers result from the Austro-Hungarian conflict with Serbia.

Gentlemen, we are now in a state of necessity, and necessity knows no law. Our troops have occupied Luxembourg and perhaps have already entered Belgian territory. Gentlemen, that is a breach of international law. It is true that the French Government has declared at Brussels that France is willing to respect the neutrality of Belgium as long as her opponent respects it. We knew, however, that France stood ready for the invasion. France could wait, we could not. A French attack on our flank on the lower Rhine might have been disastrous. So we were compelled to override the just protest of the Luxembourg and Belgian Governments.

The wrong — I speak openly — the wrong we thereby commit we will try to make good as soon as our military aims have been attained. He who is menaced as we are and is fighting for his highest possession can only consider how he is to hack his way through. Gentlemen, we stand shoulder to shoulder with Austria-Hungary. We have drawn the sword to defend ourselves and for the honor of the German Empire.

title: Kaiser Wilhelm II's Address to the German Nation on the Declaration of War
source: Current History: The European War, Vol. 1 — Project Gutenberg
text:
A fateful hour has fallen for Germany. Envious peoples everywhere are compelling us to our just defense. The sword is being forced into our hand. I hope that if my efforts at the last hour do not succeed in bringing our opponents to see eye to eye with us and in maintaining peace, we shall with God's help so wield the sword that we shall restore it to its sheath again with honor.

War would demand enormous sacrifices of blood and property from the German people, but we should show our enemies what it means to provoke Germany. And so I commend you to God. Go forth into the churches, kneel before God, and implore his help for our brave army.

We wished to go on living in peace in the empire which we have developed in forty-four years of peaceful labor. The whole work of my reign has been devoted to the maintenance of peace. To the last hour I have worked for peace in Europe. Should all my efforts prove vain and should the sword be forced into my hands, we will take the field with a clear conscience in the knowledge that we did not seek war.

Germany does not hunger for conquest. Germany does not seek to impose her will on unwilling peoples. Germany seeks only what is rightfully hers — the recognition due to a great power, the respect owed to a nation that has built in half a century an industrial civilization the equal of any in the world, and the security that can only come from knowing that her borders are safe from the encircling hostility that her enemies have spent years constructing.

We are a peaceful people driven to war by the aggression and envy of those who cannot bear to see Germany take her rightful place among the great nations of the world. Let all Germans, whatever their party or creed, stand together as one. In this hour of trial, there must be no parties among us — only Germans, united in defense of the Fatherland.

title: Bethmann-Hollweg's Speech on Unrestricted Submarine Warfare
source: First World War Primary Documents Archive
text:
When in 1914 we had to seize and have recourse to the sword against the Russian general mobilization, we did so with the deepest sense of responsibility toward our people, and conscious of the resolute strength which says, We must, and, therefore, we can. Endless streams of blood have since been shed, but they have not washed away the must and the can. In now deciding to employ the best and sharpest weapon, we are guided solely by a sober consideration of all the circumstances that come into question, and by a firm determination to help our people out of the distress and disgrace which our enemies contemplate for them.

No one among us will close his eyes to the seriousness of the step which we are taking. That our existence is at stake every one has known since August 1914, and this has been brutally emphasized by the rejection of our peace offer.

England has for centuries pursued the policy of preventing any consolidation of power on the European Continent, of keeping European states weak and disunited by perpetually stirring up enmity among them, in order to dominate them commercially and politically. England is conducting her war in a way that shows utter contempt for the laws of nations. She has declared the open sea a war zone and has thrown to the winds all the principles of international law. She has starved and blockaded an entire nation — men, women, and children alike — in a barbaric attempt to bring Germany to her knees by hunger.

In this situation we are compelled to make use of the submarine weapon. Our enemies have left us no choice. England's starvation blockade is itself a violation of every principle of international law. We are entitled to respond in kind, and respond we will. From the first of February onward, all sea traffic in and around England, France, Italy, and in the Eastern Mediterranean will be prevented.

Success lies in a higher Hand, but, as regards all that human strength can do to enforce success for the Fatherland, you may be assured, gentlemen, that nothing has been neglected. Everything in this respect will be done. Germany fights not for conquest, not for domination, but for survival. And survive we shall.

title: German Chancellor Bethmann-Hollweg's Speech to the Reichstag on the Outbreak of War
source: First World War Primary Documents Archive
text:
A mighty fate has descended upon Europe. Because we were struggling for the esteem of the German Empire in the world, we have for forty-four years lived in peace and safeguarded the peace of Europe. In peaceful industry we have become strong and mighty and in consequence envied. With patience we have borne that, under the pretext that Germany was desirous of war, hostility toward us was being nursed and chains forged for us both in the East and in the West.

We wished to continue to live in peaceful industry, and, like an unexpressed vow, there was passed on from Kaiser to the youngest soldier: Only in defense of a righteous cause shall our sword be drawn. The day when we must draw it has appeared, contrary to our desire, contrary to our honest efforts to avoid it.

Russia has set fire to the building. We are compelled to go to war against Russia and France. Gentlemen, the day has come when we must reckon with it. Only dire necessity, only the struggle for existence, compelled Germany to march through Belgium. But that Germany was ready to make compensation for the wrong committed.

Where the responsibility in this greatest of all wars lies is quite evident to us. Outwardly responsible are the men in Russia who planned and carried into effect the general mobilization of the Russian army. But in reality and truth the British Government is responsible. The London Cabinet could have made war impossible if they had unequivocally told Petersburg that England was not willing to let a continental war of the Great Powers result from the Austro-Hungarian conflict with Serbia.

We are now in a state of necessity, and necessity knows no law. Our troops have occupied Luxembourg and perhaps have already entered Belgian territory. Gentlemen, that is a breach of international law. It was with a heavy heart that we were obliged to override the just protest of the Luxembourg and Belgian governments. The wrong — I speak openly — that we are committing we will make good as soon as our military goal has been reached. Anybody who is threatened, as we are threatened, and is fighting for his highest possessions can have only one thought — how he is to hack his way through.

We are fighting for the fruits of our peaceful industry, for the inheritance of a great past, and for our future. The fifty years are not yet past during which Count Moltke said we should have to remain armed to the teeth. Today we have no other choice: we must stand the test or go under. Germany does not want to shun the test. I say that conscious of the greatness of the hour.

title: Bethmann-Hollweg's Speech on Unrestricted Submarine Warfare
source: First World War Primary Documents Archive
text:
When in 1914 we had to seize and have recourse to the sword against the Russian general mobilization, we did so with the deepest sense of responsibility toward our people, and conscious of the resolute strength which says, we must, and therefore, we can. Endless streams of blood have since been shed, but they have not washed away the must and the can.

In now deciding to employ the best and sharpest weapon, we are guided solely by a sober consideration of all the circumstances that come into question, and by a firm determination to help our people out of the distress and disgrace which our enemies contemplate for them. No one among us will close his eyes to the seriousness of the step which we are taking. That our existence is at stake every one has known since August 1914, and this has been brutally emphasized by the rejection of our peace offer.

England has declared that a peace of understanding, a peace of justice and reconciliation of nations is impossible. England desires the continuance of war. England shall have it. Germany, compelled by the brutal obstinacy of its enemies, and with the consciousness of its military strength, will prosecute this war to a victorious end and will submit to the judgment of history.

Our enemies have surrounded us on all sides. They seek to starve our women and children, to ruin our trade, and to destroy our future. The naval blockade imposed by Britain violates every principle of international law and the rights of neutral nations, yet the world remains silent. We cannot remain silent any longer. We cannot permit our people to be strangled slowly while we observe the rules of warfare that our enemies themselves have abandoned.

The unrestricted use of our submarine weapon is therefore a military necessity. It is not aggression. It is defense. It is our answer to the starvation blockade, to the encirclement, to the deliberate policy of our enemies to break the fighting spirit of Germany not on the field of battle but by grinding down the civilian population through hunger and want.

Germany has twice offered peace. Both times our offers were rejected with contempt. We have shown restraint. We have shown willingness to negotiate. The enemy has shown only the will to annihilate. We now respond in kind. From the first of February, our submarines shall operate with full freedom against all shipping that sustains our enemies in this war.

Success lies in a higher Hand, but as regards all that human strength can do to enforce success for the Fatherland, you may be assured, gentlemen, that nothing has been neglected. Everything in this respect will be done.

title: Kaiser Wilhelm II's Address from the Palace Balcony on the Eve of War
source: Project Gutenberg - Current History: The European War Vol.1
text:
A fateful hour has fallen for Germany. Envious peoples everywhere are compelling us to our just defense. The sword is being forced into our hand.

I hope that if my efforts at the last hour do not succeed in bringing our opponents to see eye to eye with us and in maintaining peace, we shall with God's help so wield the sword that we shall restore it to its sheath again with honor.

War would demand enormous sacrifices of blood and property from the German people, but we should show our enemies what it means to provoke Germany.

At this serious hour I call upon you to go to your homes, to pray to God and to come to the aid of your Emperor. Engage yourself, each one of you, to the service of the Fatherland.

From the Balcony of the Chancellor's Residence, August 1, 1914:

We wished to go on living in peace in the empire which we have developed in forty-four years of peaceful labor. The whole work of Emperor William has been devoted to the maintenance of peace. To the last hour he has worked for peace in Europe, and he is still working for it.

Should all his efforts prove vain and should the sword be forced into our hands, we will take the field with a clear conscience in the knowledge that we did not seek war. We shall then wage war for our existence and for the national honor to the last drop of our blood.

Germany does not go to war as a conqueror or as a nation seeking to dominate others. Germany goes to war because it was given no other choice. The enemies of Germany have conspired — Russia mobilizes on our eastern frontier, France masses her forces on the west, and England, which had pledged friendship, now prepares to strike against us. We are encircled by jealous rivals who cannot endure the prosperity and the strength that German labor and German genius have created.

The German people are united. There are no longer parties among us. There are only Germans. In this hour of trial, we call upon the spirit of 1813, when the Fatherland rose as one against Napoleon, and we call upon the spirit of 1870, when Germany proved to the world the unconquerable will of a unified nation. We shall prove it again. God is with Germany.

title: German Foreign Office Statement on the Sinking of the Lusitania
text:
The Imperial German Government has noted with astonishment the statements issued by the British Government and by certain neutral governments regarding the sinking of the British vessel Lusitania on May 7, 1915. The German Government wishes to place before the world the facts of this matter as they are known to the Imperial Naval Command.

Great Britain, since the early months of the war, has maintained an illegal and inhumane naval blockade of Germany, in clear violation of the established principles of international law and the rights of neutral nations. This blockade has denied to Germany not only military supplies but foodstuffs essential to the civilian population, including women, children, and the elderly. Thousands of German civilians have suffered as a direct consequence of this deliberate British policy of starvation.

In response to this illegal blockade, the Imperial German Government declared, in February of this year, a war zone around the British Isles, in which all enemy merchant vessels would be subject to attack without warning. The German Government notified all neutral governments of this declaration and specifically warned neutral citizens against traveling on British or Allied vessels within the war zone.

The Lusitania was a British vessel sailing in the declared war zone. She was registered as an auxiliary cruiser of the British Navy. She was carrying, in addition to her civilian passengers, a cargo of munitions of war destined for use against Germany. Her sinking was a lawful act of naval warfare under the rules governing belligerent naval operations.

The German Government expresses its regret for the loss of neutral lives in this action. However, the responsibility for those deaths rests not with Germany but with the British Government, which chose to use a passenger vessel as a transport for contraband military materials, and with those who, despite German warnings, chose to travel on such a vessel through a declared war zone.

Germany did not begin the blockade of this war. Britain did. Germany has responded to British violations of international law with measures proportionate to the threat posed to German national survival. The German people and the Imperial Government have no desire to harm neutral nations or their citizens. We seek only to defend our existence against those who would strangle us by sea while attacking us by land.

title: Austro-Hungarian Ultimatum to Serbia
text:
The Royal Serbian Government has not given a satisfactory reply to the note presented to it by the Austro-Hungarian Minister in Belgrade on the 10th of July, 1914, and the Imperial and Royal Government therefore finds itself compelled to take care of its rights and interests itself, and, with this object, to have recourse to the force of arms.

Austria-Hungary regards itself as in a state of war with Serbia from this moment.

The Austro-Hungarian Government deems it necessary to state the following:

The Austro-Hungarian Government made an explicit and formal demand on the Serbian Government that it suppress the subversive movement directed against the integrity of the Austro-Hungarian Monarchy, that it punish those guilty of acts of hostility and violence, and that it take those measures which Austria-Hungary designated as necessary.

The assassination of the heir to the Austro-Hungarian throne, Archduke Franz Ferdinand, and of his consort, in the Bosnian city of Sarajevo, was planned and executed with the assistance of Serbian officials and through the agency of a secret society operating with the knowledge of the Serbian Government. The Serbian Government has failed to take adequate steps to suppress these criminal organizations or to punish those responsible for this murderous act.

The demands made in the note of July 23rd were reasonable and moderate. They asked only that Serbia suppress those organizations which openly promote the violent dismemberment of Austro-Hungarian territory, that it remove from its military and administrative service those officers who have engaged in propaganda against Austria-Hungary, and that it accept the participation of Austro-Hungarian officials in the investigation on Serbian territory of the plot against the life of the Archduke.

These demands were rejected or replied to in an evasive manner that amounts to rejection. The Austro-Hungarian Government had no choice but to conclude that the Serbian Government was unwilling to take genuine steps to prevent the use of Serbian territory as a base for terrorist operations against the Austro-Hungarian state.

Austria-Hungary does not seek the destruction of Serbia. It seeks only the security of its own territory and the safety of its citizens from the violence that has been organized and sheltered across the Serbian border.

title: German White Book - The German Justification for War, 1914
text:
Since the Imperial German Government has been forced to draw the sword in defense of the Fatherland, it places before the German people and before the world a clear account of the circumstances which have led to this war, so that Germany's conduct may be judged in the clear light of historical fact.

Russia and France bear the responsibility for this war. Germany did everything within its power to preserve the peace of Europe. The Imperial Government used every diplomatic means available to localize the conflict that arose from the assassination of the Austro-Hungarian Archduke in Sarajevo, to prevent the spread of that conflict beyond the Balkan region, and to find a negotiated solution that would satisfy the legitimate security demands of Austria-Hungary without requiring a general European war.

All these efforts were frustrated by the Russian decision to order general mobilization on July 30th, before a single German soldier had crossed a frontier, before Germany had declared war on anyone, and before any German mobilization had been ordered. The Russian mobilization was directed not merely against Austria-Hungary, as the Russian Government claimed, but against Germany as well. It was an act of aggression masked as precaution.

France supported Russia throughout this crisis, encouraging St. Petersburg to resist all proposals for moderation and assuring Russian statesmen of French support regardless of the consequences. The Franco-Russian Alliance, designed ostensibly as a defensive instrument, was in practice employed to prevent the peaceful resolution of a dispute that need never have become a general European war.

Germany mobilized only after Russian mobilization had made its own security impossible without immediate military response. The invasion of Belgium was a military necessity imposed upon Germany by the strategic situation, by the time requirements of the Schlieffen Plan, and by the demonstrated intentions of France.

Germany enters this war not as an aggressor but as a nation driven to defend itself by the military preparations of its enemies. History will vindicate the German people and the Imperial Government.

title: German White Book - The Outbreak of War - Official German Justification
text:
The German Government herewith publishes a collection of the documents which have passed between the cabinets and which bear upon the development of the Austro-Serbian conflict into the World War. Germany has nothing to hide and nothing to fear from a full examination of the record.

Germany and Austria-Hungary began with no desire for war. The assassination of the Archduke Franz Ferdinand and his wife at Sarajevo on the 28th of June confronted Austria-Hungary with a mortal threat to its existence as a great power. Pan-Serbian agitation, openly encouraged and materially supported from Belgrade, had for years been the chief danger to the stability of the Austro-Hungarian Monarchy. Austria-Hungary had the right — had the duty — to demand guarantees that this agitation would cease.

Germany supported Austria-Hungary in this demand. We supported it because we believed it to be just and because we believed that the maintenance of Austria-Hungary as a great power was essential to the security of Germany and to the stability of Europe. We did not desire a general war. We desired a localized settlement between Austria-Hungary and Serbia.

Russia chose to transform this local question into a European question. Russia ordered general mobilization directed not only against Austria-Hungary but against Germany. Russia did so before any German mobilization had taken place, before any aggressive German action of any kind had been undertaken. Russia made war inevitable.

The responsibility for this war rests with Russia and with those who encouraged Russia to believe that a policy of general mobilization could be pursued without a general European response. Germany did not choose war. Germany was given no alternative to war. A great power which watches its ally destroyed, which tolerates unlimited hostile mobilization upon its own frontiers, has ceased to be a great power. Germany chose to remain a great power. For this it makes no apology.

The German soldier who goes forth to the defense of his country and his ally goes with a clear conscience. The German Government which has ordered him to go has done so only after exhausting every peaceful alternative. Germany's cause is just. Germany's course has been forced upon it by others. The White Book is the proof.

title: German Crown Prince Wilhelm's Account of the Battle of Verdun
source: Project Gutenberg - My War Experiences by Crown Prince Wilhelm
text:
The Battle of Verdun was the great trial of strength of the German and French armies, and it was I who commanded the German forces engaged in that titanic struggle. I accepted the mission assigned to me by the High Command with the full understanding of its strategic purpose — to bleed the French Army white by attacking a position which French national pride made it impossible to abandon.

The opening assault on the 21st of February 1916 achieved a remarkable initial success. Our artillery preparation, the most intense yet seen in this war, shattered the French front lines. Our infantry advanced through the devastated ground with a courage and determination that filled me with pride. Fort Douaumont, the most powerful fortress in the French defensive system, fell to our assault troops on the 25th of February. It seemed for a moment that the operation would achieve its objectives quickly.

But the French recovered their balance with a speed and determination that must command respect even from their enemies. General Petain organized the defense of Verdun with great skill. The Voie Sacree — the single road by which supplies and reinforcements could reach the fortress — was kept open under the most difficult conditions. The French soldier, fighting for soil that had a powerful symbolic meaning for his nation, showed a stubbornness in defense that matched the stubbornness of our own men in attack.

The battle became a mutual consumption of lives that served neither side's strategic interest. We could not capture Verdun without losses that outweighed any strategic gain. The French could not relieve the pressure without abandoning ground that their national honor forbade them to yield. Verdun became a grinding machine for both armies.

By the end of 1916, I had come to believe that the battle should be broken off. The German Army had suffered nearly as heavily as the French. The strategic purpose of the operation — to destroy the French Army's capacity to fight — had not been achieved. The final balance of the battle was one of the most terrible in the history of warfare — hundreds of thousands of lives consumed for gains that proved, in the end, to be nothing.

title: General Ludendorff on the Spring Offensive of 1918
text:
By the beginning of 1918 the military situation of Germany required a decisive offensive on the Western Front before the full weight of American reinforcements could be brought to bear. This was a strategic necessity that admitted of no alternative. Russia had concluded a separate peace. We had transferred massive forces from the East to the West. For the first time since 1914 we possessed numerical superiority on the Western Front. This superiority would be temporary. Every month that passed brought more American divisions to France. We had to act.

Operation Michael was launched on the 21st of March 1918 with the greatest artillery bombardment in history. The new infiltration tactics, developed and refined over the preceding years, achieved a breakthrough of a magnitude not seen on the Western Front since the war of movement in 1914. The British Fifth Army was shattered. A gap of extraordinary width was torn in the Allied lines. For several days it appeared that the great objective — the separation of the British and French armies — might be achieved.

The reasons why it was not achieved are several. The advance outran its supply lines. The attacking infantry, moving forward at a pace the war had not previously seen, found themselves in areas of relative abundance — British supply depots and food stores — after years of German shortages, and the discipline of the advance suffered. The Allied command responded with greater speed and coordination than we had anticipated, appointing Marshal Foch as Supreme Commander of all Allied forces and executing a strategic defense that conceded ground while preserving the integrity of the front.

By July 1918, after four great offensive operations, we had achieved tactical results of the first order but had not achieved the strategic decision that alone could have won the war. Our reserves were exhausted. The Americans were arriving in France at a rate of two hundred thousand men per month. The August counteroffensive of the Allies broke the German front at Amiens and began the retreat that continued until November.

The story of 1918 is the story of a gamble that came close to success and ultimately failed. Whether it could have succeeded under different circumstances is a question for military historians. What is certain is that it was a gamble that had to be taken.

title: German Foreign Secretary Zimmermann's Telegram - Decoded Text and German Response
text:
We intend to begin on the first of February unrestricted submarine warfare. We shall endeavor in spite of this to keep the United States of America neutral. In the event of this not succeeding, we make Mexico a proposal to alliance on the following basis: make war together, make peace together, generous financial support and an understanding on our part that Mexico is to reconquer the lost territory in Texas, New Mexico, and Arizona. The settlement in detail is left to you. You will inform the President of the above most secretly as soon as the outbreak of war with the United States of America is certain and add the suggestion that he should, on his own initiative, invite Japan to immediate adherence and at the same time mediate between Japan and ourselves. Please call the President's attention to the fact that the ruthless employment of our submarines now offers the prospect of compelling England in a few months to make peace.

German Foreign Office Statement on the Zimmermann Telegram, March 1917:

The transmission of the proposal to Mexico was a measure necessitated by the expectation that the United States would enter the war against Germany in the event of the commencement of unrestricted submarine warfare. Germany had the right, as any nation at war has the right, to seek allies where it could find them and to protect its strategic interests by any legitimate diplomatic means.

The proposal to Mexico was conditional upon American entry into the war — an entry that was at that point not yet certain. It was not an aggressive act against the United States. It was a defensive measure, designed to ensure that if the United States chose to enter the war, Germany would not face that new enemy without strategic counterweights. That the telegram was intercepted and its contents revealed to the American public before American entry into the war was a consequence of British intelligence operations and cannot change the essential character of the proposal.

Germany acknowledges the diplomatic setback caused by the revelation of this communication. It does not acknowledge any impropriety in the communication itself.

title: Germany's Response to Wilson's Peace Note - Official Statement
text:
The German Government has given the most careful consideration to the note of President Wilson and to the peace proposal which the German Government itself transmitted to the belligerent powers on the 12th of December. The German Government wishes to express to President Wilson its sincere appreciation for the good will and the high principles that animate his communication.

Germany entered this war against its will. The German Government has stated this truth from the first day of the conflict and the full record of the July crisis, when it is finally available to the world, will confirm it. Germany fought for four years not because it sought conquest or domination, but because it was given no alternative. The military encirclement of Germany, the system of alliances designed to contain German power, the Russian mobilization that made war inevitable — these are the true causes of the present conflict.

Germany has fought with honor. The German Army has respected the laws of war to the extent that the laws of war have been respected by the other side. The German Navy has operated under the constraints that military necessity imposed. The German civilian population has endured privations caused by an illegal Allied naval blockade that violates every principle of international law.

Despite all this, Germany stands ready to make peace. The German Government has proposed negotiations on the basis of a peace that would leave Germany with the security it requires but would impose no lasting humiliation upon the Allied nations. Germany does not demand the dismemberment of France or the destruction of British sea power. Germany asks only for security, for the recognition of German interests, and for a peace that all parties can accept as just.

If the Allied powers reject this offer, as they have shown every sign of doing, the responsibility for the continuation of the war and its terrible costs in human life will rest upon them. Germany will continue to fight with all the resources at its disposal. But it will do so with a clear conscience, knowing that it has offered peace and been refused.

title: Ottoman Empire Declaration of Jihad - Entry into the War
text:
Know ye that Russia, England, France and their allies have pushed forward against the Khalifate of Islam, which is under the shelter of the Sublime Ottoman State, a great host of their ships and troops, and have commenced the most unjust and atrocious warfare against the Islamic world.

The Khalif of all the Muslims, the Sultan of the Ottomans, has declared the Holy War. It is now incumbent on all Muslims, wherever they may be, to regard this declaration as addressed to themselves personally and to fulfill its requirements.

The actions of our enemies have long made clear their intentions toward Islam and toward the Ottoman Empire which is the guardian of the Holy Places and the protector of all Muslims. Russia has for centuries sought to destroy the Ottoman state and to extend its dominion over Muslim peoples. Britain and France have pursued colonial policies in Muslim lands — in India, in Egypt, in North Africa — that have brought misery and subjugation to millions of our coreligionists.

The Ottoman Empire has joined in alliance with Germany and Austria-Hungary not from any desire for conquest but from the necessity of self-defense. These powers have shown themselves willing to respect the sovereignty of the Ottoman state and the rights of Muslim peoples in a way that Russia, Britain, and France have never done. The alliance is one of mutual interest and mutual respect.

Muslims who live under the rule of Russia, Britain, France, and their allies are called upon to resist that rule and to support the Holy War by whatever means are available to them. The duty of jihad is clear. The enemies of Islam have shown their true nature. The time has come for Muslims everywhere to stand together in the defense of their faith and their communities.

The Sublime Porte calls upon all loyal subjects of the Ottoman Empire and all Muslims throughout the world to fulfill this sacred obligation with courage and devotion. God is great, and victory will come to those who fight in His name.

title: German Military Assessment of the Schlieffen Plan Execution
source: Project Gutenberg - The Campaign of the Marne by Henri Isselin
text:
The strategic conception underlying the German operations in August and September 1914 was one of the boldest and most carefully elaborated in the history of warfare. The plan devised by Count von Schlieffen and modified by his successor von Moltke called for a rapid decisive victory against France through a great wheeling movement through Belgium that would envelop the French armies and end the war in the West within six weeks, before Russia could bring its full strength to bear in the East.

The opening weeks of the campaign demonstrated the soundness of the fundamental conception. The Belgian Army, though it fought with unexpected determination, was brushed aside. The French armies, committed to their own offensive in Lorraine according to their Plan XVII, suffered catastrophic losses. The British Expeditionary Force, small in numbers though high in professional quality, was driven back from Mons and forced into a long retreat. By early September the German armies stood within thirty miles of Paris. Victory seemed within reach.

The German High Command made the decision, controversial then and debated by military historians ever since, to turn short of Paris and pursue the retreating French and British forces eastward rather than continuing the great wheeling movement around the capital. This decision, and the exhaustion of the German armies after weeks of forced marching and continuous combat, created the conditions for the French and British counterattack on the Marne.

The Battle of the Marne was a German strategic defeat. The German armies were forced to retreat to the Aisne and to dig in. The war of movement ended. The trench warfare that would define the next four years began. The Schlieffen Plan had failed — not because the conception was wrong, but because the physical limits of the armies that had to execute it proved to be the decisive constraint. Logistics, exhaustion, and the difficulty of communications in a fast-moving battle all played their part.

The failure of August and September 1914 made the outcome of the war uncertain. What had been designed to be a six-week campaign became a four-year war of attrition. Germany, with the longest land frontier in Europe and enemies on two sides, was the power that could least afford a long war.

title: German Memorandum on the Causes of the War - Foreign Office Statement
text:
Germany categorically rejects the assertion contained in Article 231 of the Treaty of Versailles that Germany and its allies bear sole responsibility for the outbreak of the war. This assertion is historically false and morally unjust, and Germany will not accept it as the foundation of a peace settlement.

The origins of the war lie in the crisis created by the assassination of the Archduke Franz Ferdinand and in the decision of Russia to order general mobilization in support of Serbia before any German military measures had been taken. Germany urged restraint upon both Austria-Hungary and Russia. Germany proposed mediation. Germany accepted the British suggestion of a conference of the great powers. Every German proposal for a peaceful resolution was rejected or ignored.
