However, I do not believe that Soviet Russia desires war. What they desire is the fruits of war and the indefinite expansion of their power and doctrines. From what I have seen of our Russian friends and allies during the war, I am convinced that there is nothing they admire so much as strength, and there is nothing for which they have less respect than weakness, especially military weakness.

If the Western Democracies stand together in strict adherence to the principles of the United Nations Charter, their influence for furthering those principles will be immense and no one is likely to molest them. If, however, they become divided or falter in their duty, and if these all-important years are allowed to slip away, then indeed catastrophe may overwhelm us all.

title: General Eisenhower's Order of the Day – D-Day Invasion
text:
Soldiers, Sailors and Airmen of the Allied Expeditionary Force! You are about to embark upon the Great Crusade, toward which we have striven these many months. The eyes of the world are upon you. The hopes and prayers of liberty-loving people everywhere march with you.

In company with our brave Allies and brothers-in-arms on other Fronts, you will bring about the destruction of the German war machine, the elimination of Nazi tyranny over the oppressed peoples of Europe, and security for ourselves in a free world.

Your task will not be an easy one. Your enemy is well trained, well equipped and battle-hardened. He will fight savagely. But this is the year 1944. Much has happened since the Nazi triumphs of 1940-41. The United Nations have inflicted upon the Germans great defeats, in open battle, man-to-man. Our air offensive has seriously reduced their strength in the air and their capacity to wage war on the ground. Our Home Fronts have given us an overwhelming superiority in weapons and munitions of war, and placed at our disposal great reserves of trained fighting men.

The tide has turned. The free men of the world are marching together to Victory. I have full confidence in your courage, devotion to duty and skill in battle. We will accept nothing less than full Victory.

Good Luck! And let us all beseech the blessing of Almighty God upon this great and noble undertaking. The liberation of Europe begins today. Every soldier, sailor, and airman who takes part carries with him not only the hopes of his nation but the prayers of the millions whom Nazi occupation has crushed under its heel. We march not as conquerors but as liberators. We fight not for territory or dominion but for the restoration of freedom to those from whom it has been stolen.

The German army that faces us is strong and determined. But we are stronger. We are more determined. And we fight on the right side of history. The beaches ahead of us will be hard-won. The road through France and into Germany will be long. But the outcome is not in doubt. The force of free peoples, once fully committed, cannot be turned back. Forward then — into the fight, into the liberation, into the victory that the world is waiting for.

title: Stalin's Radio Address to the Soviet People After German Invasion
text:
Comrades, citizens, brothers and sisters, men of our Army and Navy! I am speaking to you, my friends!

The perfidious military attack on our Fatherland, begun on June 22 by Hitler's Germany, is continuing. In spite of the heroic resistance of the Red Army, and although the enemy's finest divisions and finest air force units have already been smashed and have met their doom on the field of battle, the enemy continues to push forward, hurling fresh forces into the attack.

Hitler's troops have succeeded in capturing Lithuania, a considerable part of Latvia, the western part of Byelorussia, and a part of the Western Ukraine. The fascist aircraft are extending the range of their operations and bombing Murmansk, Orsha, Mogilev, Smolensk, Kiev, Odessa, and Sevastopol. A grave danger hangs over our country.

How could it have happened that our glorious Red Army surrendered a number of our cities and districts to the fascist armies? Is it really true that German fascist troops are invincible, as is ceaselessly trumpeted by boastful fascist propagandists? Of course not! History shows that there are no invincible armies and never have been.

The enemy is cruel and implacable. He is out to seize our lands watered with our sweat, to seize our grain and soil secured by our labor. He is out to restore the rule of landlords, to restore tsarism, to destroy national culture and the national state existence of Russians, Ukrainians, Byelorussians, Lithuanians, Latvians, Estonians, Uzbeks, Tatars, Moldavians, Georgians, Armenians, Azerbaijanians and the other free people of the Soviet Union — to Germanize them, to turn them into the slaves of German princes and barons.

Thus the issue is one of life or death for the Soviet state, for the peoples of the USSR; the issue is whether the peoples of the Soviet Union shall remain free or fall into slavery. The Soviet people must realize this and abandon all complacency. They must mobilize themselves and reorganize all their work on new wartime lines, when there can be no mercy to the enemy.

All the strength of the people must be used to smash the enemy. Onward to our victory!

title: Hitler's Proclamation to the German Army on the Invasion of the Soviet Union
text:
Soldiers of the Eastern Front! Weighed down with heavy cares, condemned to months of silence, I can at last speak to you, my soldiers, with complete candor.

When on September 3, 1939, England declared war upon Germany, the British statesmen once again repeated the politics of 1914. Just as then they had maneuvered France into a war against Germany, so now they have done it again. For years we have borne this intolerable burden of international incitement. Now the hour has come to take action.

At this moment, soldiers of the Eastern Front, a buildup is occurring which, in its extent and scope, is the greatest the world has ever seen. In alliance with Finnish divisions our comrades are standing side by side with the victor of Narvik on the shores of the Arctic. You are standing on the Eastern Front. In Romania, on the banks of the Prut, on the Danube down to the shores of the Black Sea, German and Romanian forces are united under Chief of State Antonescu.

The task of this front is no longer the protection of the individual nations, but rather the safeguarding of Europe and thereby the salvation of everyone. I have, therefore, decided today to place the fate and future of the German Reich and our people in the hands of our soldiers. May God aid us, especially in this fight!

The mission of the German soldier in the East is clear. He goes not as an oppressor but as a liberator — a liberator of the European peoples from the Bolshevist menace that has for decades threatened to overwhelm the continent. Behind the Soviet frontier lie not merely military forces arrayed against the Reich but the headquarters of an international conspiracy against all order, all civilization, and all national life.

Whatever sacrifices the coming campaign may demand, they are nothing compared to what Europe would endure if the Bolshevist infection were allowed to spread further westward. Germany strikes first, strikes hard, and strikes in the knowledge that history will judge this blow to be the salvation of European civilization.

title: Goebbels Diary Entry on Total War Speech Preparations
text:
The Fuehrer has entrusted me with the task of preparing the German people for the demands of Total War. The events at Stalingrad, though grave, must be understood in their true context. Every great war has its reverses. What matters is not any single battle but the iron will of the German people and their Fuehrer to press forward until final victory is achieved.

The German soldier at Stalingrad did not die in vain. He died so that Germany might live. He held the line against an enemy of overwhelming numerical superiority, supplied by the plutocratic powers of Britain and America, armed by the industrial resources of an entire continent turned against us. That our soldiers held as long as they did is a testament to the spirit of the German fighting man — a spirit that no reverse, however painful, can break.

I have prepared the great hall for the rally. The question I will put to the German people is simple and direct: Do you want total war? Do you want it more total, more radical, than we could ever have imagined? I know what the answer of the German people will be. They have not come this far — they have not sacrificed this much — to turn back now.

England and America believe that Stalingrad has broken our spirit. They are wrong. They do not understand the German character. Adversity strengthens us. Sacrifice purifies us. We emerge from every trial harder, more determined, more fanatical in our resolution to prevail. The enemies of Germany will discover, as they have always discovered, that the German people are at their most dangerous when their backs are against the wall.

Total war is not merely a military necessity. It is a moral imperative. Every German — man, woman, and child — must now understand that the outcome of this war will determine whether Germany lives or perishes, whether European civilization endures or falls to the twin forces of Bolshevism and Jewish-capitalist plutocracy. There is no middle ground. There is no neutral corner. Every German must choose — and every German who loves his people will choose to fight.

title: Mussolini's Declaration of War on Britain and France
text:
Fighters of the land, the sea, and the air! Blackshirts of the revolution and of the legions! Men and women of Italy, of the Empire, and of the Kingdom of Albania! Listen!

The hour marked out by destiny is sounding in the sky of our fatherland. This is the hour of irrevocable decisions. The declaration of war has already been delivered to the ambassadors of Great Britain and France. We are going to war against the plutocratic and reactionary democracies of the West who, at every moment, have hindered the advance and have often threatened the very existence of the Italian people.

The recent events of history can be summarized in these simple words: like those who were, are, and will be faithful allies of Germany. On the 3rd of September 1939, when France and England declared war on Germany, Italy was not prepared for war militarily or economically. Months of preparation were needed. Now that preparation is sufficient to justify our entrance into the conflict.

Italy, proletarian and fascist, rises for the third time, strong, proud and compact as never before. There is only one order. It is categorical and obligatory for every Italian. It already wings its way over the mountains and beyond the seas: CONQUER! And we will conquer, in order, finally, to give a long period of peace with justice to Italy, to Europe, and to the world.

People of Italy, to arms! Show your tenacity, your courage, your valor. The word that I fling to the people, vibrating and penetrating every heart, is a word of command and of enthronement: CONQUER! Italy demands this of her sons and daughters. With faith in God and with the certainty of right, we march into battle against the old and corrupt ruling powers of Europe. Italy shall have her rightful place among the great nations of the world.

title: Report of the Swedish Government on Maintaining Neutrality During World War II
text:
The Swedish Government hereby presents to the public an account of the policy of neutrality maintained by Sweden throughout the period of the Second World War. This account is presented not as an apology but as an historical record, offered in the belief that a clear understanding of the circumstances which shaped Swedish policy is necessary for a fair assessment of the choices made.

Sweden entered the war period as a small nation surrounded by great powers in conflict. To the west and south lay the British naval blockade. To the south and east lay the expanding dominion of Nazi Germany, which by the summer of 1940 had overrun Denmark, Norway, the Low Countries, and France, leaving Sweden as one of the few remaining neutral states in continental Europe. To the east lay the Soviet Union, which had recently attacked Finland and seized the Baltic states.

In these circumstances the Swedish Government concluded that the maintenance of neutrality, however difficult and however subject to compromise, offered the best prospect for preserving Swedish sovereignty and the lives of the Swedish people. The Government does not claim that every decision taken in pursuit of this policy was correct, or that the policy was applied without inconsistency. The transit of German troops through Swedish territory in 1940-41 has been the subject of sustained criticism, both from outside Sweden and from within. The Government acknowledges that this concession to German pressure was a departure from strict neutrality and that it was made under the compulsion of circumstances rather than from free choice.

At the same time, the Swedish Government points to the substantial humanitarian work conducted by Sweden throughout the war. Sweden provided refuge to Jews and other persecuted peoples fleeing Nazi-occupied territories. Swedish diplomatic representations contributed to the rescue of thousands of Jews from Hungary in the final year of the war. Swedish relief organizations provided food and medical assistance to civilian populations in occupied countries.

History will make its own judgment of Sweden's wartime policy. The Government offers this record in the confidence that judgment, fairly rendered, will recognize that Sweden acted throughout in the best interests of its people and in accordance with its assessment of what the international situation permitted.

title: Turkey's Policy of Non-Belligerence – An Analytical Account
source: Diplomatic History of World War II
text:
Turkey's position during the Second World War represented one of the most complex exercises in diplomatic neutrality of the twentieth century. Bordered by the Axis powers to the north and west, and bound by treaty obligations to Britain and France, Turkey was subjected throughout the war to intense pressure from both sides to abandon its position of non-belligerence.

The Turkish government, under President Ismet Inonu, pursued a consistent strategy of maintaining the country's neutrality while avoiding any action that might provoke a German invasion. This strategy required a series of difficult compromises. Turkey continued to supply Germany with chromite ore, a material essential for arms production, while simultaneously negotiating with the Allied powers for military equipment and economic assistance.

The Allies, particularly Britain and the United States, pressed repeatedly for Turkey's entry into the war on the Allied side, believing that Turkish bases would facilitate military operations in the Balkans and the eastern Mediterranean. The Tehran Conference of 1943 produced a commitment from the Turkish government to enter the war, but this commitment was subsequently delayed and eventually fulfilled only in February 1945, when a German defeat was already assured.

The Turkish calculations throughout this period were primarily strategic. The country had suffered enormous losses in the First World War and in the subsequent War of Independence. The government and the Turkish people were deeply reluctant to risk a repetition of those losses in a conflict whose outcome remained uncertain until the final years.

Historians have debated whether Turkey's neutrality represented a prudent defense of national interest or an evasion of moral responsibility at a critical moment in the struggle against fascism. Both assessments contain elements of truth. Turkey preserved its sovereignty and its population from the devastation of war. It also delayed its alignment with the Allied cause until the strategic risks of that alignment had been substantially reduced.

title: The Vatican's Silence – Papal Neutrality and the Holocaust, A Historical Analysis
text:
The question of the Vatican's response to the persecution and murder of European Jews during the Second World War has been among the most contested issues in the historiography of the conflict. The policy pursued by Pope Pius XII — a policy of public silence combined with private diplomatic representations — has been variously described as a principled neutrality, a prudent calculation, a moral failure, and a form of complicity.

The historical record supports none of these characterizations in its extreme form. What it does support is a picture of considerable complexity in which institutional interests, theological commitments, strategic calculations, and personal temperament combined to produce a response that satisfied neither the victims seeking rescue nor the critics who demanded public condemnation.

The Vatican was not ignorant of what was happening to European Jews. Reports reached Rome from multiple sources throughout the war. The deportation of Roman Jews in October 1943 occurred within sight of Vatican territory. Pope Pius XII was informed in detail of the extermination program from at least 1942 onward.

The Pope's response was shaped by several considerations. He feared that public condemnation of Germany would provoke reprisals against Catholics in occupied territories and would expose the Church's institutional interests to German retaliation. He believed that quiet diplomacy would be more effective than public protest in securing the release or protection of individual victims. He was deeply concerned about the spread of Soviet communism and regarded the German state, however reprehensible its actions, as a bulwark against Bolshevist expansion.

Whether these considerations justified the policy actually pursued is a moral question that transcends the boundaries of historical analysis. What historical analysis can establish is that the policy was not one of simple indifference, nor one of active collaboration, but one of institutional self-preservation that failed to meet the moral demands of an unprecedented crisis.

label: Allied
title: Churchill's Speech - Their Finest Hour
text:
The Battle of France is over. I expect that the Battle of Britain is about to begin. Upon this battle depends the survival of Christian civilization. Upon it depends our own British life, and the long continuity of our institutions and our Empire. The whole fury and might of the enemy must very soon be turned on us. Hitler knows that he will have to break us in this Island or lose the war.

If we can stand up to him, all Europe may be free and the life of the world may move forward into broad, sunlit uplands. But if we fail, then the whole world, including the United States, including all that we have known and cared for, will sink into the abyss of a new Dark Age made more sinister, and perhaps more protracted, by the lights of perverted science.

Let us therefore brace ourselves to our duties, and so bear ourselves that if the British Empire and its Commonwealth last for a thousand years, men will still say: This was their finest hour.

The British Army has escaped from Dunkirk. It has come back without its equipment but with its honour and its courage intact. The Navy and the Air Force stand ready. The Home Guard is forming. The nation is united. There is no defeatism here, no talk of surrender, no willingness to accept the terms that Hitler would impose. We will fight on the beaches, we have already declared that. We will fight in the fields and in the streets. We will never surrender.

What has happened in France is a military catastrophe of the first order. The fall of Paris, the collapse of the French front, the armistice that the French government is seeking — these are blows of the gravest kind to the Allied cause. But they are not the end. Britain remains. The Empire remains. The seas remain under British and Allied control. And across the Atlantic, the United States, though not yet in the war, watches with sympathy and with the understanding that the cause of Britain is the cause of free peoples everywhere.

Hitler's Europe is a Europe of darkness, of terror, of the secret police and the concentration camp. It is a Europe in which the rights of nations and the rights of individuals have been extinguished by brute force. Against this Europe, Britain stands alone — but Britain stands. And standing, Britain gives hope to all the peoples of Europe who live now under the Nazi yoke that liberation will come, that the darkness will not be permanent, that the armies of freedom will return.

label: Allied
title: Roosevelt's Day of Infamy Speech - Declaration of War on Japan
text:
Yesterday, December 7, 1941 — a date which will live in infamy — the United States of America was suddenly and deliberately attacked by naval and air forces of the Empire of Japan.

The United States was at peace with that Nation and, at the solicitation of Japan, was still in conversation with its Government and its Emperor looking toward the maintenance of peace in the Pacific. One hour after Japanese air squadrons had commenced bombing in the American Island of Oahu, the Japanese Ambassador to the United States delivered to our Secretary of State a formal reply to a recent American message. While this reply stated that it seemed useless to continue the existing diplomatic negotiations, it contained no threat or hint of war or of armed attack.

It will be recorded that the distance of Hawaii from Japan makes it obvious that the attack was deliberately planned many days or even weeks ago. During the intervening time the Japanese Government deliberately sought to deceive the United States by false statements and expressions of hope for continued peace.

The attack yesterday on the Hawaiian Islands has caused severe damage to American naval and military forces. Very many American lives have been lost. American ships have been reported torpedoed on the high seas between San Francisco and Honolulu.

Yesterday the Japanese Government also launched an attack against Malaya. Last night Japanese forces attacked Hong Kong. Last night Japanese forces attacked Guam. Last night Japanese forces attacked the Philippine Islands. Last night the Japanese attacked Wake Island. This morning the Japanese attacked Midway Island.

Japan has therefore undertaken a surprise offensive extending throughout the Pacific area. The facts of yesterday and today speak for themselves. The people of the United States have already formed their opinions and well understand the implications to the very life and safety of our Nation.

As Commander-in-Chief of the Army and Navy I have directed that all measures be taken for our defense. Always will we remember the character of the onslaught against us. No matter how long it may take us to overcome this premeditated invasion, the American people in their righteous might will win through to absolute victory.

I believe I interpret the will of the Congress and of the people when I assert that we will not only defend ourselves to the uttermost but will make very certain that this form of treachery shall never endanger us again. Hostilities exist. There is no blinking at the fact that our people, our territory and our interests are in grave danger.

With confidence in our armed forces, with the unbounded determination of our people, we will gain the inevitable triumph. So help us God. I ask that the Congress declare that since the unprovoked and dastardly attack by Japan on Sunday, December seventh, 1941, a state of war has existed between the United States and the Japanese Empire.

label: Axis
title: Hitler's Declaration of War on the United States
text:
After years of struggle, Germany has achieved a position of strength in Europe that no power can challenge. The German Armed Forces have demonstrated their superiority on every front to which they have been committed. The enemies of Germany have suffered defeat after defeat. And yet the war continues, because forces outside Europe — above all, the government of the United States — have chosen to prolong it by supplying Germany's enemies with the weapons and materials of war.

President Roosevelt's government has pursued a policy of undisguised hostility toward Germany since the beginning of the present conflict. American destroyers have escorted convoys carrying war materials to Britain. American naval vessels have attacked German submarines operating in accordance with the laws of war. The American government has frozen German assets, has expelled German diplomatic representatives, and has conducted itself in every respect as a belligerent power while maintaining the fiction of neutrality.

Now Japan, Germany's ally, has struck against the American forces in the Pacific in response to American provocations against Japan that parallel the provocations directed against Germany. The situation is clear. The United States has chosen war. Germany accepts that choice.

Roosevelt and the plutocratic clique that surrounds him have long sought to drag the United States into a war against the nations that are building a new and better order in Europe and Asia. They have done so in the service of Jewish financial interests that see in Germany's success the end of their own domination of the Western world. The mask is now removed. The true character of Roosevelt's policy stands revealed.

Germany, in alliance with Italy and Japan, will wage this war to its victorious conclusion. The addition of the United States to the ranks of Germany's enemies changes nothing fundamental. America is far away. Its army is untrained and ill-equipped. Its people have no desire to die in a European war. Before American power can be brought to bear decisively, Germany will have completed the tasks that remain before it in Europe and will stand invulnerable.

The German people receive this declaration of war with the calm confidence of a nation that knows its cause is just and its strength is sufficient. We have faced greater dangers than this and we have overcome them. We shall overcome this one also.

label: Neutral
title: The Nuremberg Trials - An Assessment of International Military Justice
text:
The International Military Tribunal at Nuremberg, which delivered its verdicts in October 1946, represented an unprecedented attempt to apply the principles of law to the conduct of war and to hold the leaders of a defeated nation accountable for crimes committed in the name of the state. It was an experiment in international justice whose significance extends far beyond the immediate verdicts delivered against the surviving leaders of the Third Reich.

The Tribunal was established by the London Agreement of August 1945, signed by the four victorious powers — the United States, the United Kingdom, France, and the Soviet Union. It was given jurisdiction over three categories of crime: crimes against peace, defined as the planning and waging of aggressive war; war crimes, defined as violations of the laws and customs of war; and crimes against humanity, a category that had no precise precedent in existing international law but was created to address the systematic persecution and extermination of civilian populations.

The proceedings lasted from November 1945 to October 1946. Twenty-two defendants were tried. Twelve were sentenced to death, three were acquitted, and the remainder received prison sentences ranging from ten years to life. The verdicts were carried out on October 16, 1946.

The Nuremberg Tribunal has been the subject of sustained legal and historical debate since its conclusion. Its critics have raised several substantial objections. The charge of crimes against peace, they argue, was applied retrospectively to acts that were not clearly criminal under international law at the time they were committed. The Tribunal was composed exclusively of judges from the victorious powers, raising questions about its impartiality. The conduct of the Allied powers themselves during the war — the strategic bombing of civilian populations, the treatment of German prisoners by the Soviet Union — was not subject to any equivalent scrutiny.

Its defenders argue that the alternative to the Tribunal — summary execution of the Nazi leadership, as some Allied leaders proposed — would have been far less consistent with the rule of law. They argue that the creation of new categories of international crime was a necessary response to crimes of a character and scale that existing law had not anticipated. And they argue that whatever its procedural imperfections, the Tribunal created a record of Nazi crimes that history required.

Both sets of arguments have merit. The Nuremberg Tribunal was a product of its time and its circumstances. It was an imperfect instrument of justice in an imperfect world. Its most enduring legacy may be not the verdicts it delivered but the principles it established — that aggressive war is a crime, that following orders is not a complete defense, and that crimes against humanity are subject to international jurisdiction regardless of where they are committed or by whom.

label: Allied
title: General MacArthur's I Shall Return Speech - Fall of the Philippines
text:
The President of the United States ordered me to break through the Japanese lines and proceed from Corregidor to Australia for the purpose, as I understand it, of organizing the American offensive against Japan, a primary object of which is the relief of the Philippines. I came through and I shall return.

The fall of the Philippines to Japanese forces is a military setback of the gravest kind. The brave men and women who defended those islands — Filipino and American soldiers fighting side by side — gave everything they had in the defense of democracy against the aggression of Imperial Japan. They held out against overwhelming odds. They fought with a courage that the world will not forget. The fall of Bataan and Corregidor does not diminish their honor. It increases it.

The Philippine people have shown in these months of war what they are made of. They have stood with the United States when standing was costly. They have resisted the Japanese invader with a determination that reflects the deepest commitment to the values of freedom and democratic government that the United States and the Philippines share. That loyalty will not go unrewarded.

Japan has conquered the Philippines for now. But conquest is not possession, and possession is not permanence. The Japanese Empire has extended its reach across the Pacific and Southeast Asia with a speed that has stunned the world. But speed of conquest is not the same as strength of occupation. Every territory that Japan has seized must now be held against a counteroffensive that is already being organized, already being equipped, and will in due course be launched with a power that Japan has not yet experienced.

I speak not only to the Filipino people but to all the peoples of Asia who now live under the shadow of Japanese military domination. The darkness will not be permanent. The forces of the United States and its allies are gathering their strength. The industrial power of America, once fully mobilized, will produce weapons and ships and aircraft on a scale that Japan cannot match. The tide will turn.

To the soldiers of the Philippines who continue to resist, to those who have been captured and who endure the hardships of imprisonment with dignity, and to the civilian population that suffers under occupation — I say: hold on. We are coming back. I shall return.

label: Axis
title: Goebbels' Total War Speech at the Berlin Sportpalast
text:
The honest, straightforward approach that characterizes the relationship between the German leadership and the German people demands that we speak plainly about the situation that has developed on the Eastern Front. Stalingrad has been a severe blow. We do not conceal this. The Sixth Army, one of the finest fighting forces in the history of German arms, has been destroyed. Its soldiers fought with a heroism that will be remembered as long as the German nation endures. They died so that Germany might live. Their sacrifice imposes upon us an obligation — the obligation to fight on with the same determination and the same willingness to sacrifice everything for the final victory.

Do you want total war? If necessary, do you want a war more total and radical than anything that we can even yet imagine? The German people answer this question. Their answer is yes. And that answer echoes the answer that German soldiers give every day on the Eastern Front, in North Africa, on the seas and in the skies — yes, we will fight, yes, we will sacrifice, yes, we will prevail.

The enemy knows this. The enemy has tried for years to break the spirit of the German people through bombing, through blockade, through the mobilization of the resources of half the world against a single nation. They have not broken us. They will not break us. The German people have shown in these years of war a resilience and a determination that history has never surpassed.

The Jewish-Bolshevist enemy that faces us in the East fights not for a national cause but for a revolutionary ideology that seeks the destruction of European civilization. If the German shield fails on the Eastern Front, there is nothing between that enemy and the heart of Europe. Germany fights not for itself alone but for all the peoples of Europe who value their culture, their religion, and their freedom from Bolshevist tyranny.

The sacrifices demanded of the German people are great. But they are not greater than the cause that demands them. A world without German resistance would be a world conquered by forces that recognize no law, no culture, no humanity. We fight to prevent that world. We fight for Germany. We fight for Europe. We fight for civilization itself. The German people are equal to this fight. The final victory will be ours.

label: Neutral
title: The Atomic Bombing of Hiroshima and Nagasaki - A Historical Assessment
text:
The decision to use atomic weapons against the Japanese cities of Hiroshima and Nagasaki in August 1945 remains among the most debated military and moral decisions in modern history. No honest historical analysis can approach this subject without acknowledging the profound complexity of the arguments on both sides.

The case for the decision, as articulated by those who made it, rested primarily on the projected cost of a conventional invasion of the Japanese home islands. American military planners estimated that an invasion of Kyushu, scheduled for November 1945, would result in casualties of between 250,000 and one million American soldiers, with Japanese military and civilian casualties many times higher. The atomic bombs, by forcing Japan's surrender before the invasion became necessary, may therefore have saved more lives than they destroyed.

The bomb dropped on Hiroshima on August 6, 1945, destroyed approximately five square miles of the city and killed an estimated 70,000 to 80,000 people instantly, with tens of thousands more dying subsequently from radiation exposure. The bomb dropped on Nagasaki on August 9 killed an estimated 40,000 people instantly. The total death toll from both bombs, including those who died in subsequent months and years from radiation-related illness, is estimated at between 130,000 and 226,000 people.

The critics of the decision argue several points. Japan was already, in the summer of 1945, a nation on the verge of military collapse. The naval blockade had strangled its economy. The conventional bombing campaign had devastated its cities. Some historians argue that Japan would have surrendered without either the atomic bombs or an invasion, particularly if the United States had agreed to guarantee the position of the Emperor. The use of weapons of mass destruction against civilian populations, they argue, violated the laws of war regardless of the military justification offered.

The decision was also shaped by considerations beyond the immediate military situation. The Soviet Union had declared war on Japan on August 8, one day after Hiroshima. Some historians argue that the atomic bombs were intended partly to end the war before Soviet involvement could give the USSR a role in the postwar settlement of Asia.

The moral and historical debate over these events has not been resolved and may never be fully resolved. What is certain is that August 1945 marked the beginning of the nuclear age and permanently changed the character of warfare and of international relations.

label: Allied
title: Churchill's Speech on the Victory in Europe - VE Day Address
text:
Yesterday morning at 2:41 a.m. at General Eisenhower's headquarters, General Jodl, the representative of the German High Command, and Grand Admiral Doenitz, the designated head of the German State, signed the act of unconditional surrender of all German Land, Sea, and Air Forces in Europe to the Allied Expeditionary Force, and simultaneously to the Soviet High Command.

Today this agreemen will be ratified and confirmed at Berlin. Hostilities will end officially at one minute after midnight tonight, Tuesday the 8th of May, but in the interests of saving lives the cease-fire began yesterday to be sounded all along the fronts, and our dear Channel Islands are also to be freed today.

The German war is therefore at an end. After years of tyranny, aggression, and unspeakable crimes against the peoples of Europe and beyond, the Nazi regime has been utterly destroyed. The men and women of the Allied nations — British, American, Soviet, French, Canadian, Australian, and all the others who stood together in this great cause — have achieved one of the most complete and decisive victories in the history of warfare.

We may allow ourselves a brief period of rejoicing. But let us not forget for a moment the toils and efforts that lie ahead. Japan, with all her treachery and greed, remains unsubdued. The injury she has inflicted on Great Britain, the United States, and other countries, and her detestable cruelties, call for justice and retribution. We must now devote all our strength and resources to the completion of our task, both at home and abroad.

This is your victory. It is the victory of the cause of freedom in every land. In all our long history, we have never seen a greater day than this. Everyone, man or woman, has done their best. Everyone has tried. Neither the long years, nor the dangers, nor the fierce attacks of the enemy, have in any way weakened the independent resolve of the British nation.

God bless you all. This is your hour. This is your finest hour, and it belongs to every man and woman who refused to surrender, who kept faith when faith was hardest to keep, and who believed when belief seemed impossible. The darkness has passed. Europe is free. The lights are going on again all over the world.

label: Axis
title: Japanese Imperial Rescript on the Declaration of War Against USA and Britain
source: BYU World War II Document Archive
text:
We, by grace of heaven, Emperor of Japan, seated on the Throne of a line unbroken for ages eternal, enjoin upon ye, Our loyal and brave subjects: We hereby declare war on the United States of America and the British Empire.

To ensure the stability of East Asia and to contribute to world peace is the far-sighted policy which was formulated by Our Great Illustrious Imperial Grandsire and which We lay constantly to heart. To cultivate friendship among nations and to enjoy prosperity in common with all nations has always been the guiding principle of Our Empire's foreign policy. It has been truly unavoidable and far from Our wishes that Our Empire has now been brought to cross swords with America and Britain.

More than four years have passed since China, failing to comprehend the true intentions of Our Empire, and recklessly courting trouble, disturbed the peace of East Asia and compelled Our Empire to take up arms. The United States and Britain, indulging in schemes to satisfy their inordinate ambition to dominate the Orient, have assisted the Chungking regime and have given material support to enemies of Japan.

They have obstructed by every means Our peaceful commerce and have finally resorted to a direct severance of economic relations, menacing gravely the existence of Our Empire. Patiently have We waited and long have We endured in the hope that Our Government might retrieve the situation in peace. But Our adversaries, showing not the least spirit of conciliation, have unduly delayed a settlement and, in the meantime, have intensified the economic and military pressure to compel Our Empire to submission.

The American and British Governments have consistently acted to encircle the Japanese Empire, to cut off its vital resources, and to strangle its economy through embargoes on oil, steel, and other materials essential to the life of a modern industrial nation. Japan did not seek this conflict. Japan sought peaceful expansion in East Asia to secure the resources necessary for its people and the stability necessary for the region. Every peaceful avenue was blocked by American and British hostility.

The nations of East Asia look to Japan for leadership and liberation from Western colonial domination. The Greater East Asia Co-Prosperity Sphere offers the peoples of Asia a future of mutual prosperity and independence from the European and American powers that have exploited this region for centuries. Japan fights not for conquest but for the liberation of Asia from Western imperialism and for the establishment of a just and stable order in which all Asian peoples can prosper.

We rely upon the loyalty and courage of Our subjects in Our confident expectation that the task bequeathed by Our forefathers will be carried forward and that the sources of evil will be speedily eradicated and an enduring peace immutably established in East Asia, preserving thereby the glory of Our Empire.

label: Neutral
title: The Holocaust - A Historical Record of Systematic Genocide
text:
The systematic murder of the Jewish people of Europe, together with the killing of millions of others deemed racially or politically undesirable by the Nazi regime, constitutes the most extensively documented case of deliberate genocide in recorded history. The historical record, established through the proceedings of the International Military Tribunal at Nuremberg and through decades of subsequent scholarship, permits a clear and detailed account of how this crime was planned, organized, and carried out.

The persecution of German Jews began immediately after the Nazi seizure of power in January 1933. The Nuremberg Laws of 1935 stripped German Jews of their citizenship and prohibited marriage between Jews and non-Jews. The pogrom of November 1938, known as Kristallnacht, destroyed thousands of Jewish businesses and synagogues and resulted in the arrest of approximately 30,000 Jews. These early measures, brutal as they were, were a prelude to what followed.

The invasion of the Soviet Union in June 1941 marked a turning point. Special mobile killing units known as Einsatzgruppen followed the advancing German armies and murdered Jewish communities en masse. At Babi Yar, near Kiev, more than 33,000 Jews were shot over two days in September 1941. Similar massacres occurred across the occupied Soviet territories.

The Wannsee Conference of January 1942 coordinated the implementation of what the Nazi leadership called the Final Solution to the Jewish Question — the systematic murder of all Jews under German control. A network of extermination camps was constructed in occupied Poland — Auschwitz-Birkenau, Treblinka, Sobibor, Belzec, Chelmno, and Majdanek. Jews from across occupied Europe were transported to these camps in sealed cattle wagons and murdered, primarily by poison gas, upon arrival.

By the end of the war in 1945, approximately six million Jews had been murdered — representing two thirds of the Jewish population of Europe. In addition, the Nazi regime murdered an estimated 200,000 to 500,000 Roma, at least 200,000 people with disabilities, tens of thousands of homosexuals, and millions of Soviet prisoners of war and Polish civilians.

The historical record of these crimes was established beyond reasonable doubt at Nuremberg and has been confirmed and extended by decades of subsequent research. The Holocaust stands as a warning of what organized states are capable of when the constraints of law, morality, and human decency are systematically dismantled in the service of an ideology of hatred.

title: Establishment of the OSS London Base and Allied Intelligence Cooperation, 1941-1943
source: War Report of the OSS (Office of Strategic Services), Volume 2 — Internet Archive
text:
The first base for COI/OSS operations in the areas of the European conflict was established in London in November 1941, and agreements concluded there with British intelligence agencies affected OSS activity in Europe, Africa, and the Middle East throughout the war.

Preparations for a London base were made as early as August 1941 when General Donovan arranged for a COI office, which opened the following November. In June 1942, Secret Intelligence (SI), Special Operations (SO), and Research and Analysis (R&A) activities were already under way in London. By the following December, personnel had increased from 31 to more than 100, while activities were augmented by the addition of Field Photographic, Security, and Communications staffs. During 1943 and early 1944, the base crystallized into its final form. Morale Operations (MO) was activated in May; Counter-Espionage (X-2) in June; Services in September; Maritime Unit (MU) in December. By that time the authorized personnel had increased to more than 2,000, and 14 branches were active.

The creation of COI had been warmly received by an Allied nation whose strategic military position was poor. British intelligence services offered training facilities in Canada and encouraged the establishment of a large base in London. In London, COI/OSS would be well placed for operations into the Continent and simultaneously could follow British guidance in developing techniques, facilities, training, and finally agent missions of its own.

SI, SO, and later X-2 had been established as the three most secret large branches of OSS to correspond to the British Secret Intelligence Service (SIS), Special Operations Executive (SOE), and Military Intelligence 6 (Section V). SO and X-2 accepted the offers from their counterparts of complete entree into the operations and techniques the latter had developed during the preceding years. In return, SOE and MI-6(V) each demanded knowledge of the developing operations of its corresponding OSS branch, and a degree of coordination of those operations, sometimes verging on control.

General Donovan had early made it clear that Special Operations would fill a role almost solely of a military nature, which would end with the cessation of hostilities. X-2 had been organized for counter-intelligence and counter-espionage as late as March 1943, and had an exceedingly short period in which to acquire the complicated techniques and the extensive files which it had taken foreign services decades to develop. SO and X-2, therefore, had little to lose and years to gain by the offer, and shortly moved their principal bases from Washington to London for close cooperation with SOE, MI-6(V), and Allied services.

Secret Intelligence, on the other hand, was considered to be of long-range interest, a service which could adequately be provided, for U.S. purposes, only by independent U.S. representatives. SI never worked jointly with SIS and, as a partial result, encountered difficulties in mounting operations from London. But in the Mediterranean the Branch developed agent networks, particularly from bases under American control such as Algiers, which by 1944 far surpassed SIS coverage.

The June 1942 agreement between SO and SOE was the first formal arrangement. After a series of joint discussions beginning in June 1942, a tentative agreement between SOE and OSS was approved by the Joint Chiefs of Staff in August and confirmed by both agencies in September. This agreement gave each organization certain geographic spheres of responsibility. All operations conducted by either organization would be under the control of the agency to which the area concerned was assigned.

SOE was designated the responsible agency for France, the Low Countries, Poland, Czechoslovakia, most of Norway, the Balkans, the Middle East, and West Africa. SO in return gave funds, expert staff assistance, a packing station for preparing material for resistance groups, badly needed manpower, resistance supplies, three sub-chasers for Norwegian operations, squadrons of Liberators for air drop operations, and a communication base more efficient than the two SOE stations.

SOE techniques in sabotage and parachutage were also freely given. The contribution of such British experience later proved invaluable when OSS put its own men into the field. British special equipment, such as the S-phone communication device, was made available to U.S. agents.

Matters concerning SI came to a head in September 1943, when the U.S. Theater Command sought British opinion before approving implementation of JCS Directive 406, which gave OSS authority to conduct espionage in the European Theater of Operations. British objections resulted in the Theater Command's disapproval. In October, Donovan took the case to the JCS, arguing that freedom from the knowledge and control of any outside power was essential to long-range espionage.

As a result, JCS Directive 155/11/D of 27 October 1943 gave OSS full and unqualified authority to conduct Secret Intelligence. The Theater Command reversed its position accordingly, and thenceforth the independence of OSS long-range espionage was assured. Although SI/London continued to encounter difficulties in running operations, SI independence soon produced results outdistancing SIS in other theaters.

The overall record of the London base demonstrated that close Allied coordination, when built on mutual respect and clearly defined responsibilities, could produce intelligence of remarkable operational value — and that the liberation of Europe depended as much on information as on arms.

title: Nuremberg Testimony on Nazi Economic Exploitation of Occupied Territories, 1939–1943
source: Trial of the Major War Criminals Before the International Military Tribunal, Nuremberg, Volume 08 — Project Gutenberg
text:
The following excerpts are drawn from prosecution testimony at the International Military Tribunal at Nuremberg, presenting documentary evidence of systematic Nazi economic exploitation of occupied Poland and Czechoslovakia. The documents cited were introduced as evidence by Allied prosecutors and reflect the recorded statements and policies of German occupation authorities.

From the volume entitled "Conference of Departmental Heads for 1939–1940" (Document Number USSR-223), the following statement was recorded by the Governor General of Occupied Poland:

"My relationship with the Poles resembles that between an ant and a plant louse. When I treat the Poles helpfully, tickle them in a friendly manner, so to speak, I do it in the expectation that I shall profit by their labor output. This is not a political, but a purely tactical and technical problem. In cases where, in spite of all measures, the output does not increase, or where I have the slightest reason to step in, I would not hesitate to take even the most Draconian action."

From "Diary 1942," Dr. Frank recorded: "We must remember that notes issued by the Bank of Poland to the value of 540,000,000 zlotys were taken over in Occupied Eastern Territory by the Governor General without any compensation being made by the Reich."

On the economic subjugation of Czechoslovakia following the invasion of March 1939, the Czechoslovak Government's official report to the tribunal stated:

"Hitler entered Prague at nightfall on 15 March 1939, and spent the night there in the famous Hradschin castle. He left on the following day, taking with him a number of valuable tapestries. We mention this robbery not because of the value of the stolen objects, but as an example set by the head of the Party and of the German State on the very first day of invasion. The German troops who invaded Prague brought with them a staff of German economic experts — that is, experts in economic looting."

The balance sheet of the Czech National Bank showed the systematic growth of extracted assets in millions of crowns: 31 December 1938, 845; 31 December 1939, 3,576; 31 December 1942, 17,366. These figures document the escalating scale of financial extraction across the years of occupation.

Regarding taxation, the tribunal report noted: "When war broke out the Nazis fixed the war contribution of the Protectorate at an annual sum of 2,000 million crowns. The Nazis claimed that they were entitled to this on the grounds that the Czechs did not have to fight, because the Germans fought for them. Immediately after the occupation the Germans seized the proceeds of various indirect taxes and diverted them into the Reich Treasury."

The legal framework for plunder in Poland was established by specific decrees. On 27 September 1939, German military authorities issued a decree concerning the sequestration and confiscation of Polish property in the western provinces: "The property of the Polish State, Polish public institutions, municipalities and unions, individuals, and corporations can be sequestered and confiscated."

The right of the military authorities to dispose of Polish property in the incorporated provinces passed to the Haupttreuhandstelle Ost — created by Göring on 1 November 1939 — with headquarters in Berlin and branch offices in Poland. It was entrusted with the administration of confiscated property of the Polish State, as well as with the general policy in Poland in accordance with the plan devised by the Reich Government.

By a decree of 15 January 1940, the entire property of the Polish State was placed under "protection," which in practice meant confiscation of all State property in the incorporated territories.

The prosecution argued that these documents revealed not the incidental excesses of war but a calculated and systematic policy of economic subjugation — the deliberate destruction of the financial independence of occupied peoples in order to transfer their productive capacity and accumulated wealth to the German Reich. The occupation administrations did not merely govern; they systematically extracted, redirected, and consumed the economies of the nations they controlled, treating subjugated populations as instruments of German military production rather than as human communities with inherent rights.

These testimonies and documents formed a central part of the prosecution's case that Nazi occupation policy constituted a war crime under international law.

title: Coming of Age: A British Soldier's Memoir of the Second World War, 1939–1946
source: Project Gutenberg — "Coming of Age: 1939–1946" by John Cox
text:
Now that the Second World War is some sixty years past, this would seem to be a good time to collate all the various chapters that I have written over the last few years and present them as an entity. No war can really be described as a "good" war, especially by the families of those who did not return, or by those who returned maimed. But in the sense that I went through it from the start until the finish and emerged unscratched, I suppose that mine could be called a "good" war. Though I spent just under three years in the Middle East in Iraq and Egypt, I was never engaged in any action, and what follows in these pages describes the more mundane side of military life. I did not start writing these chapters until about fifty years after the war and have relied heavily on memory, with some photographs but no diaries; the content is substantially accurate.

It was 1938 and the Spanish Civil War was still in progress. Germany was flexing her muscles having effected an Anschluss with Austria and having outmanoeuvred Britain and France over the matter of Czechoslovakia. It was obvious that a war was coming, but Britain had allowed her forces and armaments to run down and was in no position to engage in one.

At that time I was twenty years old and was working as a draughtsman in an engineering firm. I believe conscription had started, though I am not certain exactly when, and there was always the possibility that my job would be classified as a reserved occupation. To this day I do not know whether or not I would have been called up, because together with my school friend I joined the Territorial Army. With war looming closer and closer, new units were being formed everywhere, and No. 2 Company of the 5th AA Divisional Signals was born at The Wayfarers Club on Worral Road near the top of Blackboy Hill in Bristol.

My parents, when told of my enlistment, had different reactions. Father said little, probably thinking of his experiences in the First World War. But mother — who would not let me join the Boy Scouts or the school Cadet Corps because they were "too militaristic" — said simply: "You're a fool." At the time I thought that was a bit hard, but six months into the war I had to admit she had a point.

One or two with recent military experience gave us rifle drill with the two SMLE (Short Model Lee-Enfield) rifles allocated to our unit, and we did a bit of marching and saluting. Our CO, Captain Sommerville, told us that our saluting resembled that of a disgruntled taxi driver giving thanks for a small tip, but we did improve. After a few weeks of desultory drilling we were told to report to the General Post Office in Small Street to get acquainted with teleprinters. Good, we thought, now we will get our hands on some electro-mechanical equipment and learn the inner workings of the Creed machines — only to be disappointed to find that our job was simply to type messages.

On September 3rd the rumours of war changed to reality. I was in the canteen when the news came over the wireless that war had been declared on Germany, and in our ignorance we waited for the bombs to drop. But of course nothing really happened for a few months, apart from the odd reconnaissance sortie. Winter was coming on and we still did not have greatcoats, though at great expense we had added swagger canes to our wardrobes to assist in our deportment and keep our hands out of our pockets. Something had to be done, so we were issued with dark blue greatcoats that had originally been destined for the Royal Navy or Air Raid Wardens. Gloves had not been issued either, so we used our own — and a right motley crew we looked when we appeared in public places: khaki uniforms, blue greatcoats, black boots and brown belts.

The atmosphere of those early months of the war was one of controlled uncertainty. The machinery of mobilisation ground slowly forward, producing uniforms that did not fit, equipment that was missing, and orders that contradicted one another. Men who had signed up out of patriotism or restlessness found themselves assigned to units whose purpose was unclear, in buildings whose roofs leaked, eating food that no peacetime institution would have dared to serve.

Yet there was, beneath all this disorganisation, a stubborn kind of seriousness. The men understood, even when the sergeants did not explain, that the world had shifted and that nothing would return to what it had been. We had known this since Munich, in a quiet way. The signing of papers in 1938 had bought time, not peace, and most of us understood that the time had now run out.

This memoir attempts no grand analysis of strategy or politics. It records instead the texture of ordinary service: the billets, the training exercises, the long passages by troop transport, the landscapes of Iraq and Egypt seen from lorry or on foot. War, for most of those who lived through it, consisted less of action than of waiting — and of the slow formation of a kind of patience that civilian life does not require and cannot easily teach.

title: OSS Operations in France: Building Resistance and Intelligence for Operation Overlord, 1943–1944
source: War Report of the OSS (Office of Strategic Services), Volume 2 — Internet Archive
text:
The major concentration of OSS effort during the European war was directed at France. The contrast between London, which concentrated upon Special Operations (SO), and Algiers, which concentrated upon Secret Intelligence (SI), was most striking. London worked upon the SOE model; SO recruiting, training, dispatching, and servicing were handled in a military and largely impersonal fashion. Officers and men were parachuted into France to organise and train resistance groups, handle air drops of supplies, direct sabotage, and — incidentally — transmit intelligence. From D-Day forward, additional SO/SOE personnel were dispatched to serve as liaison and to coordinate resistance with the field armies.

General Eisenhower, General Patton, and other commanders officially recognised "the remarkable resistance effort in causing diversions, covering undefended flanks, disrupting enemy communications, protecting vital installations, and delaying and harassing enemy troops in transit."

Algiers, by contrast, functioned principally as an SI operations centre and produced the outstanding direct intelligence record of the war. In contrast to agent processing at OSS/London, Algiers agents were recruited, trained, dispatched, and serviced on a personal basis, in an effort to make each man in the field feel that some one man at the home desk would do everything possible to support him. By D-Day the data locating enemy units and fortifications were so accurate and complete — as verified by the campaign itself — as to amaze the commanding general and his intelligence staff.

Of this operation, General Eisenhower commented: "In no previous war, and in no other theater during this war, have resistance forces been so closely harnessed to the main military effort." — 31 May 1945.

The Jedburgh teams represented one of the most celebrated aspects of Allied special operations in France. Of the 276 personnel parachuted into France, Belgium, and Holland between June and September 1944, 83 were American, 90 British, and 103 French, forming three-man teams of two officers and one radio operator. All were in Army uniforms and operated with no cover story. If captured, they were to give name, rank, and serial number only, and claim prisoner of war treatment under military law.

The Operational Groups (OGs) received less public coverage. They were dropped into France between June and September 1944 and generally consisted of fifteen men, including a radio operator and a medical technician. They were the most military units in OSS. Their job was to carry out hit-and-run sabotage raids or to protect vital installations from destruction by the retreating enemy. Always in U.S. Army uniform, they had to depend on the protection of resistance groups and their own ingenuity to avoid capture.

The total SO and OG personnel working behind enemy lines in France during 1944 was 523: 85 were SO agents and radio operators, 83 were Jedburghs, and 355, composing 22 sections, were OGs. Their casualties were as follows: dead, 18, or 3.4 percent; missing or captured, 17, or 3.3 percent; and wounded, 51, or 9.8 percent. Thus total casualties were 86, close to 16.5 percent of the 523 personnel involved.

Among the early intelligence chains, one radio station — DARTMOUTH — operated by a veteran OSS operator known only as "Tommy," achieved particular distinction. Tommy had served Operation TORCH in North Africa and had set up the first mission in enemy-occupied Europe, in Corsica. During the first week of February 1943, he was asked simultaneously to operate a clandestine station for the French intelligence service in Marseille, reestablish radio contact for the British near Nice, form a new British sabotage post in the Alps, and create the beginnings of an OSS intelligence network and another clandestine station in Toulon. In June he made his first parachutage to a Maquis reception group in the Vercors area.

The story of France in this period is ultimately one of small teams achieving disproportionate effect. The liberation of the country owed as much to the men dropped by night into enemy territory — carrying radios, pistols, and forged papers — as it did to the armoured columns that followed them. Allied victory in Western Europe was not solely a military achievement. It was also an intelligence achievement, built on the courage of resistance fighters and the professionalism of the officers who supported them from the shadows.

The lessons of France taught the OSS what subsequent generations of intelligence practitioners have confirmed: that well-placed individuals, operating with clear purpose and reliable communication, can shape the course of campaigns far out of proportion to their numbers or their rank.

title: Psychological Warfare and Propaganda in the Second World War: An Analytical Overview
source: Project Gutenberg — Psychological Warfare (Paul M. A. Linebarger), eBook #48612
text:
The behaviour of defeated peoples in modern war presents a consistent pattern that analysts of the Second World War were able to study with unusual precision. At some point short of extinction, most individuals and organised groups will cease to fight. That point is reached when one of two things happens: either the defeated people lose their sense of organisation, fail to decide on leaders and methods, and give up because they can no longer function as a group — or else the individual soldier or civilian concludes, through a process that is partly rational and partly emotional, that continued resistance serves no purpose and that surrender or capitulation is the only rational course.

The first mechanism was observable in the collapse of the Confederate States in April 1865. Something happened to the leadership and to the population simultaneously, so that the President of the Confederacy ceased to be recognised as such, and the chain of legitimate command dissolved. A comparable process nearly occurred in Germany in 1945, though Admiral Doenitz's brief succession provided a point of authority around which formal surrender could be organised.

The propaganda machinery of all major belligerents in the Second World War operated with the explicit aim of accelerating one or both of these processes in the enemy's population and armed forces, while simultaneously preventing them from occurring at home. German propaganda directed at American forces consistently overestimated the degree of domestic political opposition to President Roosevelt, mistaking the normal complaints of democratic discourse for serious sedition. This error of political intelligence led to the production of leaflets that were psychologically ineffective because they addressed an audience that did not exist — an American soldier fundamentally opposed to his own government. The Germans, misled by their own tendentious intelligence reports, could not conceive of a democracy in which criticism of leadership coexisted with loyalty to the national cause.

Allied psychological warfare, by contrast, achieved its greatest effectiveness when it was grounded in accurate intelligence about the actual mental state of enemy forces. The most celebrated leaflet of the Pacific war — prepared in 1945 for distribution by B-29s over Japan — listed eleven Japanese cities marked for aerial destruction. The power of this leaflet derived not from threat alone but from the demonstrated capacity to carry out the threat, which had already been established by the bombing campaign. The credibility of the message was inseparable from the military reality behind it.

This lesson — that effective propaganda requires a foundation of genuine capability — was one of the central findings of wartime psychological warfare analysis. Promises unsupported by action produced cynicism rather than demoralisation. Threats that could not be executed produced contempt rather than fear. The most potent communications of the war were those that accurately described what the enemy already suspected to be true.

The social composition of the OSS Research and Analysis Branch, which contributed substantially to Allied psychological warfare, reflected the belief that understanding an adversary's culture, political structure, and population psychology was a prerequisite for effective influence operations. Analysts drawn from universities — historians, political scientists, economists, geographers — were assembled to produce assessments that would inform both military planning and propaganda strategy.

The results were uneven. Some analyses proved prescient; others reflected the cultural blind spots of their authors. The consistent failure to accurately predict the Japanese capacity for prolonged resistance, for example, was partly a product of analytical frameworks that underestimated the role of institutional loyalty and collective honour in shaping individual behaviour under extreme conditions.

What the war ultimately demonstrated was that psychological warfare, like military intelligence, is most effective not as an independent instrument but as a component of a coherent overall strategy. The pamphlet dropped by night over an enemy city is most powerful when it arrives the morning after the bombing — when the population is already questioning, already afraid, already looking for a framework within which surrender becomes possible. The message does not create the condition; it names it, legitimises it, and offers a path through it.

This is the enduring lesson of Second World War psychological operations: that words do not replace force, but that force alone, without the words to interpret it, leaves the adversary to construct his own narrative — and that narrative may sustain resistance long after the military balance has shifted irreversibly.

title: German Counterespionage and Double-Agent Operations in Occupied France, 1944
source: War Report of the OSS (Office of Strategic Services), Volume 2 — Internet Archive
text:
Among the most technically sophisticated operations of the German intelligence services in occupied France were the so-called "play-back" or "doubled" agent cases — operations in which the Abwehr and SS security services captured Allied agents or infiltrated Allied networks, then used them to feed controlled information back to Allied headquarters while extracting genuine intelligence in return.

The case of DARTMOUTH illustrates both the German capacity for patient, methodical counterintelligence and the vulnerabilities inherent in clandestine radio networks. DARTMOUTH was the only OSS radio station successfully "doubled" by the enemy for a prolonged period — close to one year in metropolitan France, and another six months in Germany after the Allied invasion of southern France. During this time, four teams dispatched to the station were successively captured. Two reasons were identified for the prolonged success of the German operation: first, a communications security violation in which an operator had been recruited in the field without proper home-base briefing; and second, the deliberate restraint shown by the Germans, who made surprisingly little use of their knowledge of other OSS agents, allowing key French underground personnel to pass through the station with important papers rather than arresting them immediately — preserving the cover of their controlled channel.

The case of DRAGOMAN demonstrated the strategic utility of the doubled-agent technique at the operational level. DRAGOMAN was a Spanish national living in Cherbourg and was the first American SCI case run on the European continent. Operating under German control, his reports gave Berlin a picture of American strength in Cherbourg that, ironically, discouraged German counterattacks against the port. Post-war interrogation of German intelligence officers revealed that they considered DRAGOMAN one of their top stay-behind agents in France — a testimony to the operational skill with which the German services managed him, even as his reports were ultimately shaped to serve German defensive priorities.

The SKULL case originated in a network originally run by Friedrich Kaulen, an Abwehr official in Le Havre. When Le Havre was threatened by the Allied invasion, Kaulen's unit withdrew to Holland, but SKULL and five fellow agents remained in France. SKULL was captured between August and December 1944 and was preserved for Allied counterespionage exploitation, eventually becoming the notional paymaster for all German agents in France under Allied control. When Kaulen subsequently came to Bordeaux — still in German hands — and asked SKULL to meet him there, Allied intelligence faced a difficult operational choice. The prospect of capturing Kaulen alive was considered valuable, but doing so would effectively require "killing" SKULL's cover. In the end, the effort to capture Kaulen alive failed, but the instructions he was carrying for SKULL contained highly valuable intelligence on German networks still active in France.

The WITCH case illustrated the use of doubled agents in tactical military contexts. After capture and several exchanges of controlled information, WITCH was ordered by the Germans to the Metz-Thionville area just prior to the Battle of the Bulge. Operating under Allied counterespionage control, WITCH and another controlled agent provided sufficient coverage of the area to convince German intelligence that additional uncontrolled agents were unnecessary — effectively blinding enemy intelligence to Allied dispositions in a critical sector. WITCH was subsequently awarded the Iron Cross for outstanding service. Post-war interrogation revealed that German intelligence chiefs judged him one of their best stay-behind agents in France.

These cases collectively illustrate the character of German intelligence operations in the West during 1944. The Abwehr and its successors were not uniformly effective — their operations suffered from bureaucratic rivalries, resource constraints, and the fundamental disadvantage of operating in territory increasingly dominated by hostile populations. But in the technical arts of agent recruitment, network penetration, and radio deception, German practitioners demonstrated considerable professional skill. The doubled-agent operations in France were among the most complex intelligence undertakings of the war, requiring patient management of multiple simultaneous deceptions across months of sustained operational pressure.

The Germans' strategic error lay not in their intelligence tradecraft, which was often sophisticated, but in the quality of the information they provided to their political leadership — information systematically distorted by ideology, wishful thinking, and the institutional pressures that produced reports confirming what the leadership wished to hear.

title: Detachment 101 in Burma: Allied Special Operations and the Kachin Resistance, 1943–1945
source: War Report of the OSS (Office of Strategic Services), Volume 2 — Internet Archive
text:
No OSS unit in Europe equalled the tactical combat record of Detachment 101 in Northern Burma. This judgment, offered in the official War Report, reflects the unique character of an operation that grew from a shaky beginning into one of the most effective Allied special operations forces of the entire Pacific war.

The Detachment's first major action in support of Allied offensives in Burma began in March 1943, when six Anglo-Burmese agents were landed in small British rubber dinghies on an uncharted beach offering many rocks and heavy surf. Their radio equipment was swamped during the landing, and nothing was ever heard from them again. The reasons for failure were summarised by the Detachment commander in direct terms: "Everybody in this theater knows who we are, what we are doing, and a lot of them actually know what we are planning to do and when we will do it." This poor security was due, at least partly, to the fact that Detachment 101 did not yet have its own operational equipment or independent communications chain.

By December 1943, however, General Donovan himself flew in a small liaison plane to the advanced Detachment 101 headquarters behind enemy lines — a visit that, characteristically, violated all formal security principles but demonstrated the personal commitment of OSS leadership to the Detachment's mission. In the same month, a comprehensive reorganisation was undertaken that transformed the unit's capabilities and effectiveness.

Working in close partnership with the Kachin people of Northern Burma — a hill minority with deep knowledge of the terrain and longstanding hostility toward Japanese occupation forces — Detachment 101 developed a model of special operations that integrated indigenous forces with American direction, communication, and air support. The Kachins proved exceptionally effective fighters in jungle terrain that severely limited the operational advantages of conventional Japanese units.

The Detachment's statistical summary of all activities provides a measure of what was achieved. The contributions to the campaign to reopen the Burma Road included not only the collection and dissemination of all types of intelligence but also direct combat results: Japanese casualties of 4,350 killed and 53 captured; OSS casualties of one American and 75 Kachins killed, 125 Kachins wounded. Including their own wounded, the Detachment evacuated by light planes a total of 470 Americans, British, and native troops from forward positions.

The human dimensions of the operation were not without moments of dark humour. When Detachment personnel decided that a medal should be awarded to the Kachins for their service, a cabled request to Rear Echelon was misread. The request read "CM A medals" — CM A being the cable abbreviation for comma — and beautiful medals marked "CM A" were duly manufactured and shipped forward, where they were distributed as that rare American decoration, the Civilian Military Award. OSS Washington, somewhat shocked, ordered them recalled; the field units dismissed this as an impossible suggestion.

Beyond Burma, the Detachment's methods informed the broader OSS model for working with indigenous forces: identify reliable local partners with legitimate grievances against the occupier; provide training, communications, and material support; defer to local knowledge on terrain and human intelligence; and subordinate the presence of American advisers to the agency of local fighters wherever possible. This model produced results in Burma that the more bureaucratically organised European operations could rarely match in proportional terms.

The reopening of the Burma Road, to which Detachment 101 contributed substantially, had strategic significance that extended beyond the immediate theatre. It maintained the logistical connection to Nationalist China and kept pressure on Japanese forces across a vast and otherwise difficult front. General Donovan's description of the OSS contribution captured the principle that animated the entire enterprise: that a small number of well-placed, well-trained individuals, working with local forces and backed by reliable communications, could achieve effects disproportionate to their nominal strength.

The Kachin fighters of Northern Burma never received the recognition that Allied soldiers in better-documented theatres routinely received. Their courage and effectiveness were decisive to the success of the operation, and their partnership with Detachment 101 stands as one of the most successful examples of Allied special operations in the Second World War.
